> - **Topic:** Introduction to Computational Fluid Dynamics, Governing Equations of Fluid Flow and Heat Transfer, Conservative Form of Navier-Stokes Equation, General Transport Equation, Physical and Mathematical Classifications of Partial Differential Equations, Elliptic, Parabolic and Hyperbolic Equations, Comparison of Experimental, Theoretical and Numerical Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)?

**Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is the branch of fluid mechanics that uses **numerical analysis and algorithms** (primarily on digital computers) to solve and analyze problems involving **fluid flows**. It involves the discretization of the governing partial differential equations of fluid dynamics into a system of algebraic equations that can be solved numerically to obtain approximate solutions for velocity, pressure, temperature, and other field variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Verbatim)**: "CFD is the science of determining a numerical solution to the governing equations of fluid flow and heat transfer (continuity, momentum, and energy equations) by progressing the fluid flow from one spatial location to another as a function of time (if transient) or by iteratively refining the solution until the steady-state conditions are satisfied."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Virtual Wind Tunnel

Imagine you are an aerospace engineer designing a new aircraft wing. In the 1950s, you would have had **two options**:
1. **Build a physical model** and test it in a wind tunnel (expensive, slow, limited data points).
2. **Solve simplified theoretical equations** (fast, but only works for simple shapes).

**CFD is the third option** — a *virtual wind tunnel* sitting inside your computer. You draw the wing, the computer "blows virtual air" over it by solving millions of equations, and you get detailed flow information at *every point* in space — pressure, velocity, turbulence, even the tiniest vortex behind the trailing edge. Just as a pilot uses a flight simulator before flying the real plane, an engineer uses CFD to "test" a design before manufacturing the prototype.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students**: CFD does *not* replace theory or experiment — it **complements** them. Theory gives the laws, experiments give the truth, and CFD fills the gap by providing detailed, cost-effective data where neither is practical.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Fluid Dynamics

Every fluid flow problem is governed by **three fundamental conservation laws** that CFD ultimately solves:

| Pillar | Conservation Principle | Resulting Equation |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **1. Mass** | Mass is neither created nor destroyed | Continuity Equation |
| **2. Momentum** | Force = rate of change of momentum (Newton's 2nd Law) | Navier–Stokes Equations |
| **3. Energy** | Energy is neither created nor destroyed (1st Law of Thermodynamics) | Energy Equation |

### 1.4 Key Physical Parameters (Dimensionless Numbers)

The behavior of fluid flow is universally characterized by several **dimensionless numbers**. These appear repeatedly in CFD analysis:

- **Reynolds Number (Re)**: $\displaystyle Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ — Ratio of inertial to viscous forces; determines whether flow is laminar or turbulent.
- **Mach Number (Ma)**: $\displaystyle Ma = \frac{U}{c}$ — Ratio of flow speed to local speed of sound; classifies flow as subsonic, sonic, or supersonic.
- **Prandtl Number (Pr)**: $\displaystyle Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k}$ — Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
- **Grashof Number (Gr)**: $\displaystyle Gr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ — Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces in natural convection.
- **Nusselt Number (Nu)**: $\displaystyle Nu = \frac{h L}{k}$ — Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams**: "**Re**al flows need **Re**ynolds to predict **Re**gime" — *Re* ≪ 1 (creeping), *Re* ~ 1 (laminar), *Re* ≫ 10⁵ (turbulent).

### 1.5 Why CFD? — Engineering Motivation

| Engineering Field | CFD Application |
|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Aerospace** | Wing design, rocket nozzle flow, re-entry vehicle aerothermodynamics |
| **Automotive** | Aerodynamic drag reduction, cabin ventilation, engine combustion |
| **Civil** | Wind loading on tall buildings, bridge pier scour, dam spillways |
| **Biomedical** | Blood flow through arteries, airflow in lungs, drug delivery |
| **Energy** | Wind turbine blade design, solar chimney, nuclear reactor cooling |
| **Process** | Mixing tanks, chemical reactors, cyclone separators |
| **Marine** | Hull resistance, propeller cavitation, ship-stern flow |
| **HVAC** | Room air distribution, smoke management, cleanroom design |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Governing Equations

### A. Conservation of Mass — The Continuity Equation

The principle of mass conservation states that the **net mass flux** out of an infinitesimal control volume equals the rate of **decrease of mass** within it. The differential form for a Newtonian fluid is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

For **incompressible flow** ($\rho = \text{const}$), this simplifies to the divergence-free condition:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

### B. Conservation of Momentum — Navier–Stokes Equations

Newton's second law applied to a fluid element gives the **Cauchy momentum equation**, which for a Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity becomes the **Navier–Stokes equation**:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}
$$

In expanded Cartesian form, the x-component is:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x
$$

### C. Conservation of Energy — The Energy Equation

The first law of thermodynamics applied to a fluid element gives:

$$
\rho c_p \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla T \right) = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + \dot{q}
$$

where $\Phi$ is the **viscous dissipation function** and $\dot{q}$ is the volumetric heat source.

## 2.2 The General Transport Equation (The Unifying Formulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most important equation in CFD**: All three conservation laws can be written as a **single generic equation** known as the General Scalar Transport Equation. Every CFD code (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, COMSOL) is fundamentally solving this form.

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi
$$

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t}$ | **Transient / Unsteady** term (rate of accumulation) |
| $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi)$ | **Convective** term (transport by bulk motion) |
| $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi)$ | **Diffusive** term (transport by molecular diffusion) |
| $S_\phi$ | **Source / Sink** term (generation or destruction of $\phi$) |

By choosing $\phi$, $\Gamma_\phi$, and $S_\phi$ appropriately, you recover any conservation law:

| $\phi$ | $\Gamma_\phi$ | $S_\phi$ | Resulting Equation |
|--------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **1** (mass) | **0** | **0** | Continuity Equation |
| $u$, $v$, $w$ | $\mu$ | $-\partial p/\partial x_i + \rho g_i$ | Navier–Stokes (momentum) |
| $T$ | $k/c_p$ | $\Phi/c_p + \dot{q}/c_p$ | Energy Equation |
| $k$ (TKE) | $\mu + \mu_t/\sigma_k$ | $G_k - \rho \epsilon$ | $k$-equation (turbulence) |

## 2.3 Conservative vs. Non-Conservative Form

> [!NOTE]
> **Conservative Form** uses the **divergence of flux** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$), which guarantees that **mass, momentum, and energy are exactly conserved** across the entire domain. This is the form used by all production CFD codes.

**Non-conservative form** uses material/particle derivatives, which is convenient for analysis but **violates conservation** at discrete grid points.

### Conservative Form of Navier–Stokes (x-component):

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho u w)}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xz}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x
$$

Compactly:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nabla \cdot \bar{\bar{\tau}}_x + \rho g_x
$$

## 2.4 Classification of Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)

A general second-order linear PDE in two independent variables $x$ and $y$ is:

$$
A \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + B \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} + C \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + D \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + E \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + F \phi + G = 0
$$

**Classification is based on the discriminant** $B^2 - 4AC$:

| Discriminant $B^2 - 4AC$ | PDE Type | Information Propagation | Example in CFD |
|---------------------------|-----------|------------------------|----------------|
| $B^2 - 4AC < 0$ | **Elliptic** | All directions (boundary-driven) | Steady incompressible viscous flow, Laplace/Poisson |
| $B^2 - 4AC = 0$ | **Parabolic** | One direction (marching in time) | Unsteady diffusion, boundary layers |
| $B^2 - 4AC > 0$ | **Hyperbolic** | Two directions (wave-like) | Inviscid compressible flow, shock waves |

### Canonical Examples

| Type | Canonical Equation | CFD Example |
|------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Elliptic** | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ (Laplace) | Pressure-Poisson equation in incompressible flow |
| **Parabolic** | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}$ | Transient heat conduction, boundary-layer momentum |
| **Hyperbolic** | $\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}$ | Acoustic wave, inviscid compressible flow, shallow water |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Rapid Revision)

| # | Equation / Concept | Compact Form | Application |
|---|---------------------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | Continuity (general) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Mass conservation |
| 2 | Continuity (incompressible) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Incompressible flows |
| 3 | Navier–Stokes (vector) | $\rho \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Viscous Newtonian flow |
| 4 | Energy equation | $\rho c_p \dfrac{DT}{Dt} = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot q$ | Heat transfer |
| 5 | **General transport equation** | $\dfrac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$ | **Unified CFD form** |
| 6 | Viscous stress (Newtonian) | $\tau_{ij} = \mu \left( \dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i} \right) - \dfrac{2}{3} \mu \delta_{ij} \nabla \cdot \vec V$ | Momentum flux |
| 7 | PDE classifier | $B^2 - 4AC$ | Type identification |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho U L / \mu$ | Flow regime |
| 9 | Mach number | $Ma = U / c$ | Compressibility |
| 10 | Prandtl number | $Pr = \mu c_p / k$ | Thermal/momentum diffusivity |

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Transport Equation

The general transport equation is derived by applying the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** to any extensive property $N = \int_V \rho \phi \, dV$ over a fixed control volume.

**Starting Point — Reynolds Transport Theorem**:

$$
\frac{dN_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

This theorem states that the rate of change of property $N$ in the system equals the rate of accumulation within the control volume plus the net outflow across the control surface.

**Step 1** — Apply the conservation principle: the system rate of change equals the net effects of **flux across the surface** and **sources/sinks** within the volume:

$$
\frac{dN_{sys}}{dt} = \int_{CS} (\text{flux of } \phi) \cdot \hat{n} \, dA + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV
$$

**Step 2** — The total flux of $\phi$ across the control surface consists of two parts:

- **Convective flux**: $\rho \phi \vec{V}$ (carried by bulk fluid motion)
- **Diffusive flux**: $-\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi$ (carried by molecular diffusion, e.g., Fourier's law for heat, Newton's law for momentum)

Therefore, total flux $= \rho \phi \vec{V} - \Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi$.

**Step 3** — Substitute this flux into the surface integral:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA - \int_{CS} \Gamma_\phi (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 4** — The two surface integrals in Step 3 must equal the accumulation term. By the Divergence (Gauss) Theorem, a surface integral over a closed surface $CS$ equals the volume integral of the divergence over the enclosed volume $CV$:

$$
\int_{CS} \vec{F} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot \vec{F} \, dV
$$

**Step 5** — Applying this to both surface integrals:

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) \, dV - \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) \, dV - \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 6** — For a **fixed control volume**, the time derivative can be taken inside the integral:

$$
\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) - \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) - S_\phi \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 7** — Since this must hold for **arbitrary** control volume, the integrand itself must vanish (this is the fundamental lemma of calculus of variations):

$$
\boxed{ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi }
$$

This is the **General Scalar Transport Equation** — the cornerstone of all CFD.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuity Equation

The continuity equation is obtained by setting $\phi = 1$, $\Gamma_\phi = 0$, $S_\phi = 0$ in the general transport equation:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho \cdot 1)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \cdot 1) = \nabla \cdot (0 \cdot \nabla 1) + 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Conservative Navier–Stokes Equation

Starting from the **non-conservative (convective) form** of the x-momentum equation:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 1** — Expand the left side using product rule on $\rho$:

$$
\rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla u = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 2** — Add and subtract $u \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ to introduce the $\rho u$ term:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} - u \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla u = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 3** — Combine the last two terms using the identity $\rho \vec{V} \cdot \nabla u = \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) - u \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})$:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} - u \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) - u \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 4** — Group the terms containing $u$:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) - u \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 5** — The bracketed term is the **continuity equation**, which equals zero:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 6** — The viscous term $\mu \nabla^2 u$ can be written as $\nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u)$ (for constant $\mu$):

$$
\boxed{ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u) + \rho g_x }
$$

This is the **conservative form** of the x-momentum equation. It is "conservative" because flux terms appear as $\nabla \cdot (\text{flux})$, ensuring global conservation.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Conservative Energy Equation

Starting from the non-conservative form:

$$
\rho c_p \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial T}{\partial z} \right) = k \nabla^2 T + \Phi + \dot q
$$

Following the same procedure as Section 3.3 (using product rule and continuity), the conservative form is:

$$
\boxed{ \frac{\partial (\rho c_p T)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} c_p T) = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + \dot q }
$$

## 3.5 Verification — Recovering Navier–Stokes from Transport Equation

For x-momentum, choose $\phi = u$, $\Gamma_\phi = \mu$, $S_\phi = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \rho g_x$:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) = \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u) + \left( -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \rho g_x \right)
$$

This matches the conservative Navier–Stokes equation derived in Section 3.3. ✓

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — PDE Classification

| PDE Type | $B^2 - 4AC$ | Characteristics Curves | Boundary Conditions | Numerical Method | CFD Example |
|----------|-------------|-------------------------|---------------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Elliptic** | $< 0$ | No real characteristics (complex) | Required on **all boundaries** of closed domain | Iterative (Gauss-Seidel, SOR, multigrid) | Pressure Poisson, incompressible flow |
| **Parabolic** | $= 0$ | One real characteristic | Required on **open boundary + initial condition** | Marching in time (explicit/implicit schemes) | Transient diffusion, boundary layer |
| **Hyperbolic** | $> 0$ | Two real characteristics | Required at **inlet only** + downstream extrapolation | Method of Characteristics, upwind schemes | Compressible inviscid flow, shocks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well-Posedness Theorem**: A PDE problem is *well-posed* if (i) a solution exists, (ii) it is unique, and (iii) it depends continuously on data. **Elliptic** problems need closed boundary data, **parabolic** need initial + open boundary, and **hyperbolic** need initial + inflow boundary. Specifying wrong boundaries gives meaningless results.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CFD Solution Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Problem Definition] --> B[Mathematical Model: Governing PDEs + BCs + ICs]
    B --> C[Selection of Discretization Method FVM / FDM / FEM]
    C --> D[Grid Generation Structured / Unstructured / Hybrid]
    D --> E[Discretization of Governing Equations Algebraic System]
    E --> F[Solver Selection SIMPLE / PISO / Coupled]
    F --> G[Solution Iteration Convergence Check]
    G --> H{Converged?}
    H -- No --> I[Refine Grid or Adjust Under-Relaxation]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Post-Processing Contours / Vectors / Iso-surfaces]
    J --> K[Validation against Experimental / Analytical Data]
    K --> L{Acceptable?}
    L -- No --> M[Re-examine Model and Repeat]
    M --> B
    L -- Yes --> N[Final Engineering Interpretation and Report]
```

## 4.2 Unification of Conservation Laws via Transport Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TE["General Transport Equation\n d(rho.phi)/dt + div(rho.V.phi) = div(Gamma.grad.phi) + S_phi"] --> C1
    TE --> C2
    TE --> C3
    TE --> C4

    C1["phi = 1, Gamma = 0, S = 0\nContinuity Equation\n drho/dt + div(rho.V) = 0"]
    C2["phi = u, Gamma = mu, S = -dp/dx + rho.gx\nNavier-Stokes x-momentum\n Conservative Form"]
    C3["phi = v, Gamma = mu, S = -dp/dy + rho.gy\nNavier-Stokes y-momentum\n Conservative Form"]
    C4["phi = T, Gamma = k/cp, S = Phi + q\nEnergy Equation\n Conservative Form"]
```

## 4.3 PDE Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Second-Order Linear PDE\n A.phi_xx + B.phi_xy + C.phi_yy + ... = 0] --> S1[Compute Discriminant D = B squared minus 4AC]
    S1 --> S2{D less than zero?}
    S1 --> S3{D equal to zero?}
    S1 --> S4{D greater than zero?}

    S2 -- Yes --> P1[ELLIPTIC\n Steady Viscous Flow\n Pressure Poisson]
    S3 -- Yes --> P2[PARABOLIC\n Transient Diffusion\n Boundary Layer]
    S4 -- Yes --> P3[HYPERBOLIC\n Wave Propagation\n Compressible Flow]
```

## 4.4 Comparison of Three Approaches — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXP[Experimental Approach]
        E1[Physical Model] --> E2[Wind Tunnel / Test Rig]
        E2 --> E3[Instrumentation Probes / PIV / LDA]
        E3 --> E4[Direct Measurement of Variables]
        E4 --> E5[Highest Accuracy for Real Flows]
    end

    subgraph THE[Theoretical Approach]
        T1[Governing PDEs + Simplifying Assumptions] --> T2[Analytical Mathematics]
        T2 --> T3[Closed-Form Solution]
        T3 --> T4[Insight into Physics]
        T4 --> T5[Limited to Simple Geometries]
    end

    subgraph NUM[Numerical CFD Approach]
        N1[Governing PDEs] --> N2[Discretization on Grid]
        N2 --> N3[Algebraic Equations]
        N3 --> N4[Iterative / Time-Marching Solver]
        N4 --> N5[Approximate Field Solution at All Points]
    end

    EXP --- THE
    THE --- NUM
    NUM --- EXP
```

## 4.5 Discretization Method Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PDESET[Governing PDEs] --> FDM
    PDESET --> FVM
    PDESET --> FEM

    FDM[FDM: Finite Difference Method\n Taylor Series Expansion\n Simple, Efficient, Structured Grids]
    FVM[FVM: Finite Volume Method\n Conservation via Control Volume\n Industry Standard for CFD]
    FEM[FEM: Finite Element Method\n Weak Form / Variational\n Complex Geometries, Structural CFD]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define CFD. List any four advantages of CFD over conventional experimental and theoretical methods.**

**Model Answer**:
**CFD Definition**: Computational Fluid Dynamics is the analysis of systems involving fluid flow, heat transfer, and associated phenomena such as chemical reactions using computer-based numerical simulation.

**Four Advantages**:
1. **Cost Effective**: Reduces the number of expensive physical prototypes and wind tunnel tests.
2. **Comprehensive Data**: Provides complete field information (pressure, velocity, temperature) at *every point* in the domain, unlike experiments which give point-wise measurements.
3. **Handles Complexity**: Can simulate flows in complex geometries (combustion chambers, biomedical flows) where analytical solutions are impossible.
4. **Parametric Flexibility**: Allows rapid testing of multiple design variations, boundary conditions, and operating scenarios without re-manufacturing.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the General Transport Equation used in CFD. Explain the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer**:
**General Transport Equation**:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi
$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term**:

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| **Transient / Unsteady** | $\partial(\rho \phi)/\partial t$ | Rate of accumulation of $\phi$ inside the control volume (non-steady storage) |
| **Convective** | $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V \phi)$ | Net transport of $\phi$ by bulk fluid motion across the control surface |
| **Diffusive** | $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi)$ | Net transport of $\phi$ by molecular diffusion (Fick's / Fourier's / Newton's law) |
| **Source** | $S_\phi$ | Net rate of generation (positive) or destruction (negative) of $\phi$ per unit volume |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below. Each contains sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **conservative form of the Navier–Stokes equation** (x-component) starting from the non-conservative form. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the Reynolds Transport Theorem, **derive the general scalar transport equation** governing CFD. Hence show how the continuity, momentum, and energy equations can each be obtained as special cases. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Conservative Form of N-S Equation

**Given (non-conservative x-momentum)**:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 1** — Compact vector notation:

$$
\rho \frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 2** — Expand the material derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \rho \vec V \cdot \nabla u + u \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 3** — Use the product rule on the convective term: $\rho \vec V \cdot \nabla u = \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V u) - u \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V)$:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V u) - u \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V) + u \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 4** — Group the last two terms:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V u) - u \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V) \right] = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 5** — The bracket is the **continuity equation = 0**:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V u) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 6** — Rewrite $\mu \nabla^2 u = \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u)$ for constant viscosity:

$$
\boxed{ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V u) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u) + \rho g_x }
$$

This is the **conservative x-momentum equation**. Similarly, by symmetry, the y and z components can be written by replacing $u$ with $v$ and $w$, and $g_x$ with $g_y$ and $g_z$ respectively.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of General Transport Equation

**Starting Point — Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)**:

$$
\frac{dN_{sys}}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec V \cdot \hat n) \, dA
$$

**Step 1** — The system rate of change of $\phi$ equals the sum of (i) net efflux across CS, and (ii) sources inside CV:

$$
\frac{dN_{sys}}{dt} = \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec V \cdot \hat n) \, dA - \int_{CS} \Gamma_\phi (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat n) \, dA + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV
$$

**Step 2** — Equating both expressions for $dN_{sys}/dt$ and bringing all to one side:

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \phi (\vec V \cdot \hat n) \, dA - \int_{CS} \Gamma_\phi (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat n) \, dA - \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 3** — Apply Gauss Divergence Theorem to convert surface integrals to volume integrals:

$$
\int_{CS} \vec F \cdot \hat n \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot \vec F \, dV
$$

So:

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \phi \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V \phi) \, dV - \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) \, dV - \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV = 0
$$

**Step 4** — Combine under a single volume integral (CV is fixed, so time derivative enters the integral):

$$
\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V \phi) - \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) - S_\phi \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 5** — Since CV is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish:

$$
\boxed{ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi }
$$

**Step 6** — Special Cases:

| Equation | Choice of $\phi$ | $\Gamma_\phi$ | $S_\phi$ |
|----------|------------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Continuity** | $\phi = 1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **x-Momentum** | $\phi = u$ | $\mu$ | $-\partial p/\partial x + \rho g_x$ |
| **Energy** | $\phi = T$ | $k/c_p$ | $\Phi/c_p + \dot q/c_p$ |

Hence, all three fundamental conservation laws are *unified* by the general transport equation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Classify second-order linear PDEs into elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic with the help of the discriminant $B^2 - 4AC$. For each class, state the **canonical equation**, the **required boundary/initial conditions for well-posedness**, and **one example from fluid dynamics**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **experimental, theoretical, and numerical (CFD) approaches** in fluid dynamics with respect to accuracy, cost, time, flexibility, and the type of information obtained. Justify with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — PDE Classification

**General Second-Order Linear PDE**:

$$
A \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + B \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} + C \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + D \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + E \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + F \phi + G = 0
$$

**Discriminant**: $D_d = B^2 - 4AC$

| $D_d$ | Type | Canonical Equation | Required Conditions | CFD Example |
|-------|------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| $< 0$ | **Elliptic** | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ (Laplace) | Boundary values on the **entire closed boundary** of the domain | Steady incompressible viscous flow, pressure Poisson equation |
| $= 0$ | **Parabolic** | $\partial \phi/\partial t = \alpha \partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2$ | Initial condition + boundary values on the **open (inflow)** end | Transient heat conduction, boundary-layer flow (Blasius) |
| $> 0$ | **Hyperbolic** | $\partial^2 \phi/\partial t^2 = c^2 \partial^2 \phi/\partial x^2$ | Initial conditions + boundary values on the **upstream (inflow)** boundary only | Compressible inviscid flow, acoustic waves, shock waves |

**Physical Interpretation**:
- **Elliptic**: Boundary value problem (BVP); disturbances propagate instantaneously in *all* directions, so the entire boundary must be specified.
- **Parabolic**: Initial-boundary value problem (IBVP); disturbances propagate in *one* direction (forward in time), so only one boundary + initial condition needed.
- **Hyperbolic**: Initial value problem (IVP); disturbances propagate at finite speed along *two* characteristic directions, so upstream boundary is sufficient.

#### Part (b) — Comparison of Three Approaches

| Criterion | Experimental | Theoretical | Numerical (CFD) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Accuracy** | Highest (real flow physics) | Highest (exact, when solvable) | Approximate (depends on mesh, model, scheme) |
| **Cost** | Very high (model + rig + instrumentation) | Low (pen + paper) | Moderate (compute hardware + software) |
| **Time** | Long (fabrication + setup + testing) | Moderate to long (for hard problems) | Moderate (depends on grid + convergence) |
| **Geometric Flexibility** | Limited (rig must be built) | Very limited (simple geometries only) | High (any complex geometry) |
| **Data Type** | Point-wise measurements (probes) | Functional form (closed expression) | Full field data at every grid point |
| **Parametric Studies** | Very expensive | Difficult for complex flows | Trivial — change input, re-run |
| **Repeatability** | Affected by noise, environment | Perfect | Perfect (deterministic) |
| **Boundary Conditions** | Set by apparatus | Idealized (analytical tractability) | User-specified (full control) |
| **Example** | Wind tunnel test of airfoil at Re = 10⁶ | Blasius solution for flat plate | ANSYS Fluent simulation of rocket nozzle flow |
| **Limitations** | Tunnel wall interference, probe disturbance | Only simple geometries and flows | Numerical diffusion, convergence issues, model errors |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: A common student mistake is stating "CFD is more accurate than experiment" or "CFD replaces experiments". Both are **wrong** and will attract **negative marks** (typically -1 to -2 per occurrence). The correct statement is: "**CFD complements experiments; experiment remains the ultimate validation standard.**"

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential for Last-Hour Revision Before KTU University Exam**

- ✅ **CFD** = Numerical solution of fluid-flow governing equations on a discretized grid.
- ✅ The **three fundamental conservation laws** of fluid flow are: **mass, momentum, energy**.
- ✅ The **General Transport Equation** is the *unified* form: $\partial(\rho \phi)/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec V \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$.
- ✅ Choosing $\phi$ = 1, $u$, $v$, $w$, $T$ gives **continuity, x-, y-, z-momentum, and energy** equations.
- ✅ **Conservative form** uses $\nabla \cdot (\text{flux})$ and guarantees **exact global conservation** — always used in CFD codes.
- ✅ **Non-conservative (convective) form** uses $\partial \phi/\partial t + \vec V \cdot \nabla \phi$ — convenient for analysis but not for discretization.
- ✅ **PDE Classifier**: Compute $B^2 - 4AC$ from the second-order coefficients.
  - **$< 0$ → Elliptic** (Laplace, Poisson) → Steady viscous flow → all boundaries needed.
  - **$= 0$ → Parabolic** (diffusion eq.) → Transient diffusion → IC + one open boundary.
  - **$> 0$ → Hyperbolic** (wave eq.) → Compressible flow → IC + inflow boundary.
- ✅ **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** is the *starting point* of every conservation-law derivation in CFD.
- ✅ The **Gauss Divergence Theorem** converts surface integrals to volume integrals — a key step in deriving the transport equation.
- ✅ **Experimental approach** is the *gold standard* for accuracy but expensive; **theoretical** gives exact insight for simple cases; **CFD** fills the gap for complex real-world problems.
- ✅ CFD does **not replace** experiments — it **complements** them; experiments are still required for final **validation**.
- ✅ **Non-dimensional numbers**: $Re$ (laminar/turbulent), $Ma$ (compressibility), $Pr$ (thermal/momentum), $Nu$ (convection).
- ✅ **Discretization methods** in CFD: **FDM** (Finite Difference), **FVM** (Finite Volume — industry standard), **FEM** (Finite Element).
- ✅ The **marching nature** of parabolic and hyperbolic PDEs means information propagates in *one direction*; elliptic problems require *global iterative* solution.
- ✅ Always state **boundary conditions and initial conditions** explicitly when solving a PDE — wrong BCs = wrong solution, regardless of solver accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of CFD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT415 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of CFD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CFD Applications

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is the branch of fluid mechanics that uses **numerical methods and algorithms** (primarily finite difference, finite volume, and finite element methods) to solve the governing equations of fluid flow — the **Navier–Stokes equations**, **continuity equation**, and **energy equation** — over a discretized domain called the **computational mesh** or **grid**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, **applications of CFD** refer to the practical engineering and scientific problem domains where numerical simulation of fluid flow, heat transfer, and related transport phenomena is used as a **design, analysis, and optimization tool**, often replacing or complementing experimental and theoretical approaches.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The course PESFT415 places CFD applications as the motivating framework for the entire module. Students must be able to identify *where*, *why*, and *how* CFD is applied across mechanical, aerospace, biomedical, civil, chemical, and energy engineering disciplines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an architect designing a **giant skyscraper**. Before pouring a single tonne of concrete, you wouldn't simply guess how the wind will push against the building. Instead, you would build a **miniature scale model**, place it in a **wind tunnel**, and observe how the air swirls around it. CFD is essentially the **virtual wind tunnel** — but on a computer.

Instead of physical air and a physical model, you:

- **Divide** the region of interest (the air around the skyscraper) into millions of tiny **cells** (the *mesh*).
- **Assign** the fundamental physical laws of motion to each cell as small algebraic equations.
- **Solve** all these equations *simultaneously* on a computer to predict pressure, velocity, temperature, and turbulence in every cell.

The end result is a **color-coded, three-dimensional map** of how the fluid behaves — telling you whether your design will survive a cyclone, how hot your CPU will get, or how a Boeing wing will lift an A380.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Applications" matter before the math?** KTU examiners frequently (40–50% of Module 1 marks) test whether students can *link a real-world engineering problem to the correct CFD methodology* (laminar vs. turbulent, steady vs. unsteady, incompressible vs. compressible, single-phase vs. multiphase). Understanding applications FIRST builds the intuition for the math that follows.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics Used in CFD Applications

The following **non-dimensional numbers** and standard metrics must be memorized as they are recurring in KTU questions:

| Metric | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | $Re$ | Inertial vs. viscous forces | $>2300$ → turbulent |
| **Mach Number** | $Ma$ | Flow speed vs. sound speed | $>0.3$ → compressible |
| **Prandtl Number** | $Pr$ | Momentum vs. thermal diffusivity | Air ≈ **0.71** |
| **Nusselt Number** | $Nu$ | Convective vs. conductive heat transfer | Higher → better cooling |
| **Skin Friction Coefficient** | $C_f$ | Wall shear stress / dynamic pressure | Aerospace optimization KPI |
| **Pressure Coefficient** | $C_p$ | Local static pressure deviation | Aerodynamic design KPI |
| **Drag Coefficient** | $C_D$ | Total drag / dynamic pressure × area | Automotive/F1 design KPI |
| **Lift Coefficient** | $C_L$ | Lift / dynamic pressure × area | Wing/airfoil design KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Atmospheric Constants (KTU standard value set):**
> - Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Dynamic viscosity of air: $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$
> - Speed of sound at 15°C: $a = 340.3 \text{ m/s}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Grand Classification of CFD Applications

CFD applications can be classified along **four orthogonal axes** — the KTU-recommended way to remember them:

1. **By Engineering Discipline** — Aerospace, Mechanical, Civil, Chemical, Biomedical, etc.
2. **By Physical Phenomenon Modeled** — External flow, internal flow, multiphase flow, reacting flow, heat transfer, etc.
3. **By Industry Sector** — Automotive, energy, electronics, healthcare, sports, environment, etc.
4. **By Flow Regime** — Incompressible/compressible, laminar/turbulent, steady/unsteady, Newtonian/non-Newtonian.

### 2.2 Detailed Application Domains

#### 2.2.1 Aerospace Engineering (Most Heavily Tested in KTU)

- **Aircraft Aerodynamics** — Design of wings, fuselage, control surfaces, flaps, slats. Predicts **lift ($C_L$)**, **drag ($C_D$)**, **lift-to-drag ratio ($L/D$)**, and **stall angle**.
- **Helicopter Rotors** — Unsteady 3D rotating-frame CFD, predicts blade-vortex interaction (BVI), noise, and vibration.
- **Rocket Nozzles** — Compressible, often supersonic flow; predicts thrust, base pressure, and nozzle wall heat load.
- **Spacecraft Re-entry** — Hypersonic flow (Ma > 5) with real-gas effects, ablation, and shock-boundary layer interaction.
- **Wind Turbine Blades** — Predicts annual energy production (AEP) and structural fatigue loading.

#### 2.2.2 Automotive Engineering (Second-Most Tested)

- **External Aerodynamics** — Drag reduction on car bodies; one of the most famous CFD stories is the *2014 Formula 1 aerodynamics revolution*, where complex vortex generators (CFD-designed) on front wings produced massive downforce.
- **Underbody and Diffuser Flow** — Ground-effect cars use CFD-optimized venturi tunnels.
- **Engine Combustion (CFD-Combustion)** — In-cylinder fuel-air mixing, knock prediction, emissions.
- **HVAC of Cabin** — Passenger thermal comfort analysis.
- **Brake Cooling** — Forced convection analysis of disc brakes.

#### 2.2.3 Turbomachinery (Mechanical Core for KTU)

- **Pumps, Compressors, Turbines** — Flow through impellers, volutes, and diffusers.
- **Steam Turbine Blade Cooling** — Internal and external cooling passages.
- **Centrifugal Pump Cavitation** — Predicts NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) margin.
- **Gas Turbine Combustors** — Reacting flow CFD with turbulence-chemistry interaction.

#### 2.2.4 Civil and Environmental Engineering

- **Wind Loading on Buildings and Bridges** — Pedestrian-level wind comfort, structural wind loads.
- **Urban Airflow and Pollution Dispersion** — City-scale CFD modeling.
- **Dam Spillways and Hydraulic Structures** — Free-surface flow simulation.
- **Sewer and Pipe Network Flow** — Wastewater engineering.

#### 2.2.5 Biomedical Engineering (Emerging KTU Hot-Topic)

- **Blood Flow in Arteries** — Aneurysm risk prediction, stent design.
- **Airflow in Human Respiratory System** — Sinus, trachea, nasal cavity CFD for surgical planning.
- **Heart Valve Dynamics** — FSI (Fluid-Structure Interaction) analysis.
- **Drug Delivery Aerosol Tracking** — Inhaler design optimization.
- **Ventilator and CPAP Design** — Especially relevant post-COVID-19.

#### 2.2.6 Electronics Cooling (Industry 4.0 Hot Area)

- **PCB and Chip Cooling** — Forced air, liquid cooling, heat sink optimization.
- **Data Center HVAC** — Server rack inlet temperature uniformity.
- **Smartphone Thermal Management** — Predicts hotspot formation.

#### 2.2.7 Marine and Offshore

- **Ship Hull Hydrodynamics** — Wave resistance, wake field, propeller-hull interaction.
- **Submarine Maneuvering** — Cavitation analysis.
- **FPSO and Offshore Platform Wave Loads** — Coupled CFD + Morison equation.

#### 2.2.8 Energy Sector

- **Wind Farm Layout Optimization** — Wake interaction between turbines.
- **Solar Collector Flow** — Heat transfer fluid optimization.
- **Nuclear Reactor Coolant Analysis** — Loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) simulation.
- **Oil & Gas Multiphase Flow in Pipelines** — Slug flow, wax deposition.

#### 2.2.9 Sports Engineering (Modern Edge)

- **Cycling, Swimming, F1 Helmets, Cricket/Soccer Ball Aerodynamics** — Drag prediction, swing/seam analysis.
- **Running Shoe Midsole Airflow** — Thermal comfort.
- **Ski and Surfboard Hydrodynamics** — Wetted-surface analysis.

### 2.3 The Engineering "Workflow" of a Typical CFD Application

Regardless of the domain, **every CFD application** follows the same six-step KTU framework:

1. **Define the Geometry** — CAD model of the physical object/region.
2. **Create the Mesh** — Subdivide the domain into finite cells/elements.
3. **Specify Physics and Boundary Conditions** — Inlet velocity, outlet pressure, wall temperature, etc.
4. **Solve the Governing Equations** — Iteratively, using numerical schemes (SIMPLE, PISO, coupled).
5. **Post-Process Results** — Velocity vectors, pressure contours, streamlines, $Nu$, $C_D$, $C_L$.
6. **Validate and Refine** — Mesh independence study, comparison with experimental data.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for CFD Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT reference for Module 1 KTU questions.** Memorize the formulas, units, and the "What does it tell us?" column.

| # | Formula | Application Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | All flow applications | Determines laminar vs. turbulent |
| 2 | $Ma = \dfrac{V}{a}$ | Aerospace, gas dynamics | Determines incompressible vs. compressible |
| 3 | $C_D = \dfrac{2 F_D}{\rho V^2 A}$ | Automotive, aerospace, sports | Quantifies drag for design optimization |
| 4 | $C_L = \dfrac{2 F_L}{\rho V^2 A}$ | Aerospace, wind turbines | Quantifies lift generated |
| 5 | $Nu = \dfrac{h L}{k}$ | Heat transfer (electronics, turbines, biomedical) | Quantifies convective heat transfer |
| 6 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$ | All heat transfer applications | Property of fluid |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho V A$ | Turbomachinery, pipelines | Mass flow rate balance |
| 8 | $\Delta p = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Pipe flow, HVAC | Pipe friction loss (Darcy-Weisbach) |
| 9 | $Q = h A \Delta T$ | All thermal CFD | Convective heat transfer rate |
| 10 | $t_{res} = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Reactors, environmental CFD | Residence time distribution |
| 11 | $y^+ = \dfrac{y u_\tau}{\nu}$ | Turbulent wall-bounded flows | First-cell height for turbulence modeling |
| 12 | $C_f = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2}$ | Aerodynamics, turbomachinery | Wall shear stress metric |
| 13 | $\epsilon_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{disp}}{V_{ideal}}$ | Combustion CFD | Combustion efficiency |
| 14 | $L/D = C_L / C_D$ | Aircraft design | Aerodynamic efficiency KPI |
| 15 | $E_{shaft} = \dot{m} \Delta h_{isentropic}$ | Turbines, compressors | Ideal work extraction/compression |

> [!NOTE]
> **In the table above, the vertical bar inside $L/D$** is rendered as a forward slash to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In the actual KTU answer sheet, write it as $L/D$ or $C_L / C_D$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility Summary

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU board examiner often asks: "Pick any ONE application of CFD and explain its role in modern engineering."** A model answer should follow this 4-point structure:
>
> 1. **Domain and Problem Statement** — What engineering problem?
> 2. **Why CFD over Experiment?** — Cost, time, safety, parametric studies.
> 3. **Governing Physics** — Which equations are involved? Incompressible/compressible? Laminar/turbulent? With/without heat transfer?
> 4. **Output KPIs** — What design decisions does it inform?
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard:** Every "derive" or "show that" question in Module 1 is rooted in either the **Reynolds number decomposition** (for turbulence) or the **continuity and Navier–Stokes equations applied to a specific application**. Below, we exhaustively derive three foundational results that are repeatedly tested in KTU board papers.

---

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Establishing the Reynolds Number from the Navier–Stokes Equations (Application: Pipeline Flow)

> **Starting Point:** The incompressible Navier–Stokes momentum equation in vector form:
> $$\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}$$

**Step 1 — Non-Dimensionalization.** Introduce dimensionless variables (denoted with a tilde):
- Length: $x = L \tilde{x}$, where $L$ = characteristic length
- Velocity: $V = U_\infty \tilde{V}$, where $U_\infty$ = characteristic velocity
- Time: $t = \dfrac{L}{U_\infty} \tilde{t}$
- Pressure: $p = \rho U_\infty^2 \tilde{p}$ (inertial scaling)
- Gravity: $\mathbf{g} = g \tilde{\mathbf{g}}$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Navier–Stokes equation.**

The convective term becomes:
$$\rho \left( \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V} \right) = \rho \left( U_\infty \tilde{\mathbf{V}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{L} \tilde{\nabla} \right) U_\infty \tilde{\mathbf{V}} = \dfrac{\rho U_\infty^2}{L} \left( \tilde{\mathbf{V}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{V}} \right)$$

The unsteady term becomes:
$$\rho \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} = \rho \dfrac{U_\infty}{L/U_\infty} \dfrac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} = \dfrac{\rho U_\infty^2}{L} \dfrac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{\partial \tilde{t}}$$

The pressure gradient becomes:
$$-\nabla p = -\dfrac{1}{L} \rho U_\infty^2 \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{p}$$

The viscous term becomes:
$$\mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{V} = \mu \dfrac{1}{L^2} U_\infty \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{V}} = \dfrac{\mu U_\infty}{L^2} \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{V}}$$

The body force becomes:
$$\rho \mathbf{g} = \rho g \tilde{\mathbf{g}}$$

**Step 3 — Divide the entire equation by the common inertial factor $\rho U_\infty^2 / L$.**

This yields the dimensionless form:
$$\dfrac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{V}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{V}} = -\tilde{\nabla} \tilde{p} + \dfrac{\mu}{\rho U_\infty L} \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{V}} + \dfrac{g L}{U_\infty^2} \tilde{\mathbf{g}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the dimensionless groups.**

The coefficient of the viscous term is the **reciprocal of the Reynolds number**:
$$\dfrac{\mu}{\rho U_\infty L} = \dfrac{1}{Re}$$

The coefficient of the body force is the **Froude number** squared inverse:
$$\dfrac{g L}{U_\infty^2} = \dfrac{1}{Fr^2}$$

**Step 5 — Final dimensionless Navier–Stokes equation:**
$$\dfrac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{V}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{V}} = -\tilde{\nabla} \tilde{p} + \dfrac{1}{Re} \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{V}} + \dfrac{1}{Fr^2} \tilde{\mathbf{g}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation for the application (pipeline flow).**

For pipeline flow, gravity is often neglected, leaving:
$$\dfrac{\partial \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{\partial \tilde{t}} + \tilde{\mathbf{V}} \cdot \tilde{\nabla} \tilde{\mathbf{V}} = -\tilde{\nabla} \tilde{p} + \dfrac{1}{Re} \tilde{\nabla}^2 \tilde{\mathbf{V}}$$

- When $Re \ll 1$ (small pipe, viscous fluid), the viscous term dominates → **Stokes flow** (creeping flow).
- When $Re \gg 1$ (large pipe, fast flow), the viscous term is negligible → **Euler flow** (inviscid), but with a thin **boundary layer** near walls.

This explains why CFD for pipeline design must correctly identify $Re$ before choosing a turbulence model.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Continuity Equation for a Control Volume (Application: Combustion Chamber Mass Balance)

> **Starting Point:** Conservation of mass in integral form for a control volume (CV):
> $$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 1 — Interpretation.**
- The first term is the **rate of accumulation** of mass inside the CV.
- The second term is the **net mass efflux** through the control surface (CS).

**Step 2 — Apply to a steady combustion chamber** (typical CFD application in rocket or jet engine). For a steady flow:
$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0$$

So the equation reduces to:
$$\int_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem and localize** to a point using the divergence theorem:
$$\int_{CS} \rho (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}) \, dV = 0$$

Since the CV is arbitrary, the integrand must vanish at every point:
$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}) = 0$$

This is the **pointwise continuity equation** — the cornerstone of every CFD solver.

**Step 4 — Expand using the product rule:**
$$\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}) = \rho \nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \rho = 0$$

**Step 5 — Incompressible specialization.**

If density is constant ($\nabla \rho = 0$), the equation reduces to:
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$$

In Cartesian coordinates, expanded:
$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Application numerical example.**

A combustion chamber has 12 fuel injectors each supplying $\dot{m}_{inj} = 0.05$ kg/s and 4 oxidizer injectors each supplying $\dot{m}_{ox} = 0.10$ kg/s. Mass leaves through a single nozzle of cross-section $A = 0.02$ m² with velocity $V_{exit}$. Find $V_{exit}$ assuming $\rho_{exit} = 5$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**
$$\dot{m}_{in} = 12(0.05) + 4(0.10) = 0.6 + 0.4 = 1.0 \text{ kg/s}$$

For steady flow, $\dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}_{in}$:
$$\rho_{exit} V_{exit} A = 1.0$$
$$V_{exit} = \dfrac{1.0}{5 \times 0.02} = 10 \text{ m/s}$$

> This is a **classic 7-mark KTU Part B question** worth remembering verbatim.

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) for Turbulent Applications (Application: Aircraft Wing)

> **Motivation:** In aerospace CFD, $Re > 10^6$ is routine. Resolving all eddies (DNS) is impossible. We **decompose** the flow into mean and fluctuating parts.

**Step 1 — Reynolds Decomposition.**
$$u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$$

where $\overline{u_i}$ is the time-averaged velocity and $u_i'$ is the fluctuation with $\overline{u_i'} = 0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the incompressible Navier–Stokes equation** (ignoring gravity):
$$\rho \dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + \rho u_j \dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the decomposition and take a time-average.**

Using the identity $\overline{\overline{u_i} \cdot \overline{u_j}} = \overline{u_i}\,\overline{u_j}$ and $\overline{\overline{u_i} u_j'} = 0$:

$$\rho \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \rho \overline{u_j} \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\dfrac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \rho \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 4 — Define the Reynolds Stress Tensor.**
$$\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

The RANS equation becomes:
$$\rho \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \rho \overline{u_j} \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\dfrac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \mu \dfrac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{ij}^{R}}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 5 — Closure Problem.**

We now have **more unknowns than equations** — the 6 components of $\tau_{ij}^{R}$ are unknown. This is the famous **closure problem** of turbulence. It is "closed" by introducing a turbulence model, e.g.:

- **Boussinesq Hypothesis (k–ε, k–ω models):**
$$\tau_{ij}^{R} = \mu_t \left( \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) - \dfrac{2}{3} \rho k \delta_{ij}$$
where $\mu_t$ is the **eddy (turbulent) viscosity** and $k$ is the turbulent kinetic energy.

- **Reynolds Stress Model (RSM):** Directly solves transport equations for all 6 $\tau_{ij}^{R}$.

**Step 6 — Application to aircraft wing CFD.**

A typical wing chord $c = 1.5$ m, free-stream $V_\infty = 70$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s). Find $Re_c$:
$$Re_c = \dfrac{1.225 \times 70 \times 1.5}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.1 \times 10^6$$

Since $Re_c \gg 5 \times 10^5$, flow is fully turbulent. The CFD code MUST use a turbulence model (typically **SST k–ω** for aerospace) — laminar or inviscid assumptions are physically invalid here. **This is the KTU examiner's classic trap question.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The CFD Application Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram is the **single most important visual summary** for the topic "Applications of CFD." It tells the student — *based on the engineering problem at hand, which CFD approach should be chosen?* This is exactly the type of "decision-tree" question KTU examiners love to ask.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Problem] --> B{External or Internal Flow?}
    B -- External --> C{Compressible?}
    B -- Internal --> D{Carrying Heat?}

    C -- No Ma is less than 0.3 --> E[Aerodynamics, Automotive, Sports]
    C -- Yes Ma greater than 0.3 --> F[Aerospace, Rockets, Supersonic Wind Tunnels]

    D -- No --> G[Pipe Networks, Pumps, Turbomachinery, Ventilation]
    D -- Yes --> H{Is phase change present?}

    H -- No --> I[Electronics Cooling, Heat Exchangers, Engine Cooling]
    H -- Yes --> J[Boilers, Condensers, Refrigeration, Power Plants]

    E --> K[Common CFD Tools - k-omega SST, Realizable k-epsilon, DES]
    F --> L[Common CFD Tools - k-omega SST, Roe Scheme, AUSM, RSM]
    G --> M[Common CFD Tools - Standard k-epsilon, RNG, RSM, Laminar]
    I --> N[Common CFD Tools - k-omega SST, V2F, Natural Convection Models]
    J --> O[Common CFD Tools - Eulerian Multiphase, VOF, Phase Change Models]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: CFD Application Workflow (Six-Stage Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[1 Geometry and CAD] --> step2[2 Mesh Generation]
    step2 --> step3[3 Physics and Boundary Conditions]
    step3 --> step4[4 Solver Run]
    step4 --> step5[5 Post Processing]
    step5 --> step6[6 Validation and Refinement]
    step6 -- Iterative loop back --> step2
    step5 -- Engineering KPI like CD or Nu --> KPI[Design Decision Output]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Classification Tree of CFD Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[All CFD Applications] --> aero[Aerospace]
    root --> auto[Automotive]
    root --> mech[Mechanical and Turbomachinery]
    root --> civil[Civil and Environmental]
    root --> bio[Biomedical]
    root --> elec[Electronics and Data Centers]
    root --> energy[Energy Sector]
    root --> marine[Marine and Offshore]
    root --> sport[Sports Engineering]
    root --> chem[Chemical Process]

    aero --> aero1[Wings, fuselage, rotors, nozzles, reentry]
    auto --> auto1[Car body, F1 aerodynamics, underbody, HVAC, brake cooling]
    mech --> mech1[Pumps, turbines, compressors, IC engine]
    civil --> civil1[Wind on buildings, dam spillways, urban dispersion]
    bio --> bio1[Blood flow, respiratory, drug delivery, heart valves]
    elec --> elec1[PCB cooling, data center HVAC, smartphone thermal]
    energy --> energy1[Wind farm, solar, nuclear coolant, oil-gas]
    marine --> marine1[Ship hull, submarine, offshore platform]
    sport --> sport1[Bicycle, ball swing, swimsuits, ski]
    chem --> chem1[Reactors, mixers, separators, distillation]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Typical Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CFD Software Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input[Pre Processing]
        i1[CAD Geometry - STEP or IGES]
        i2[Surface Cleanup and Defeaturing]
        i3[Volume Meshing - Tet or Hex or Poly]
        i4[Boundary Zone Assignment]
    end

    subgraph solver[Numerical Solver]
        s1[Governing Equations - NS, Energy, Species]
        s2[Turbulence Model Selection - k-omega SST etc]
        s3[Discretization - Finite Volume Method]
        s4[Linear System Solver - AMG or GMRES]
        s5[Time Integration - Steady or Transient]
    end

    subgraph output[Post Processing]
        o1[Velocity and Pressure Contours]
        o2[Streamlines and Vectors]
        o3[KPI Extraction - CD, CL, Nu, mdot]
        o4[Animation for Unsteady Cases]
    end

    input --> solver --> output
    o3 --> K[KPI driven Design Decision]
```
