## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are short-answer, definition, or one-line derivations. They test **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each answer should be 3–4 sentences or a single equation.

---

#### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any six major applications of CFD in mechanical and aerospace engineering."**

**Model Answer (Memorize-Ready):**

The six major applications are:

1. **Aircraft wing and fuselage aerodynamic design** — predicting lift, drag, and stall behavior before wind-tunnel testing.
2. **Rocket nozzle and combustor flow analysis** — high-Mach compressible CFD for thrust optimization.
3. **Automotive external aerodynamics** — drag coefficient ($C_D$) reduction in road cars and Formula 1.
4. **Turbomachinery (pumps, compressors, turbines)** — internal flow, cavitation, and efficiency prediction.
5. **IC engine in-cylinder flow and combustion** — fuel-air mixing, knock, and emissions prediction.
6. **HVAC and building ventilation** — indoor airflow, thermal comfort, and contamination control.

---

#### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define the Reynolds number. Why is it the most important non-dimensional number in CFD applications?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reynolds number** is defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:
$$Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

It is the most important non-dimensional number in CFD applications because:

- It **determines the flow regime** — laminar ($Re < 2300$ in pipes), transitional, or turbulent ($Re > 4000$).
- It governs the **choice of turbulence model** — laminar solver, k–ε, k–ω SST, or RSM.
- The **same value of $Re$ in geometrically similar systems produces dynamically similar flow** — enabling scale-model CFD validation.
- It directly controls the **boundary layer behavior**, separation, and pressure drop — all of which are central KPIs in aerospace, automotive, and pipeline applications.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions on Module 1 carry 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer EITHER the entire Question A OR the entire Question B (full choice). Avoid mixing between A and B in your answer sheet.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**A(a) [7 Marks]:** "Explain in detail the role of CFD in aerospace applications. Discuss the governing equations and the typical non-dimensional parameters used."

**A(b) [7 Marks]:** "A passenger aircraft is flying at a cruising speed of 250 m/s at an altitude of 10000 m. The chord length of the wing is 3.0 m. Calculate the Reynolds number and Mach number of the flow over the wing. Use standard atmospheric properties at 10000 m: $\rho = 0.4135$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.458 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $a = 299.5$ m/s. State whether the flow is compressible and whether a turbulence model is mandatory. Justify your answer."

---

**Model Solution:**

**A(a) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

CFD plays a **transformative role in aerospace engineering** by enabling detailed numerical simulation of flows that are often impossible, dangerous, or extremely expensive to test experimentally (e.g., hypersonic re-entry, full-scale aircraft icing).

**Governing equations used in aerospace CFD:**

1. **Continuity equation:** $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}) = 0$
2. **Momentum equation (Navier–Stokes):** $\rho \dfrac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}$
3. **Energy equation:** $\rho C_p \dfrac{DT}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + \dot{q}_{solar}$
4. **Equation of state (ideal gas):** $p = \rho R T$ (since aerospace flow is compressible)

**Typical non-dimensional parameters:**

- **Mach number** $Ma = V/a$ — governs compressibility and shock formation.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ — governs boundary layer and turbulence.
- **Pressure coefficient** $C_p = (p - p_\infty) / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2)$ — surface pressure distribution.
- **Lift coefficient** $C_L = 2 L / (\rho V^2 A)$ — wing lift performance.
- **Drag coefficient** $C_D = 2 D / (\rho V^2 A)$ — aerodynamic drag.

**Aerospace applications include:** wing and fuselage design, helicopter rotor analysis, rocket nozzle optimization, hypersonic re-entry thermal protection, and spacecraft plume impingement.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for role, 3 marks for equations, 2 marks for non-dimensional parameters, 1 mark for applications list.)*

---

**A(b) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.4135 \times 250 \times 3.0}{1.458 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{310.125}{1.458 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.127 \times 10^{7} \approx 2.13 \times 10^7$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Mach number.**

$$Ma = \dfrac{V}{a} = \dfrac{250}{299.5} = 0.835$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion on compressibility.**

Since $Ma = 0.835 > 0.3$, the flow is **definitely compressible**. The CFD solver **must** use a compressible formulation (variable density, energy equation coupled). Shock waves MAY form locally over wing components at this Mach number (transonic regime).

**Step 4 — Conclusion on turbulence model.**

Since $Re = 2.13 \times 10^7 \gg 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **fully turbulent**. A turbulence model is **mandatory**. The most appropriate choice for aerospace wing flows is the **SST k–ω model**, which combines the near-wall accuracy of k–ω with the free-stream robustness of k–ε. Laminar flow assumption is **physically invalid** here.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Trap):** Students often forget to multiply chord length × density × velocity correctly. Many write $Re = 2.13 \times 10^5$ (off by a factor of 100). Re-derive carefully. Marks lost: **2/7** for arithmetic, **1/7** for forgetting to state the turbulence model choice.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**B(a) [7 Marks]:** "Discuss the role of CFD in biomedical engineering. Provide at least four specific examples with the governing physics in each case."

**B(b) [7 Marks]:** "Water at 20°C flows through a pipe of diameter 0.05 m at a velocity of 2.5 m/s. Calculate the Reynolds number and determine the flow regime. If the pipe is bent at a 90° elbow of radius 0.10 m, what additional CFD analyses are required at the bend? Properties of water: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m·s)."

---

**Model Solution:**

**B(a) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

CFD has become **indispensable in biomedical engineering** because *in vivo* measurements are often impossible, dangerous, or unethical, and CFD offers a non-invasive numerical "window" into the human body.

**Four specific examples:**

1. **Blood flow in arteries (Aneurysm and Stenosis analysis).**
   - **Physics:** Incompressible, laminar (mostly), non-Newtonian (blood is shear-thinning), pulsatile.
   - **Governing equations:** Navier–Stokes + Carreau-Yasuda viscosity model + FSI coupling for arterial wall elasticity.
   - **CFD Output:** Wall Shear Stress (WSS), Oscillatory Shear Index (OSI), plaque formation risk.

2. **Airflow in human nasal cavity and respiratory tract.**
   - **Physics:** Incompressible, laminar-to-transitional, steady or transient, heat transfer (air conditioning by turbinates).
   - **Governing equations:** Navier–Stokes + energy equation.
   - **CFD Output:** Pressure drop, velocity profiles, particle deposition for inhaled drug delivery.

3. **Heart valve dynamics (mechanical and bioprosthetic).**
   - **Physics:** Unsteady, multi-phase (blood + leaflet), moving boundary (FSI).
   - **Governing equations:** ALE (Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian) formulation of Navier–Stokes.
   - **CFD Output:** Flow recirculation, hemolysis risk, leaflet fatigue loading.

4. **Inhaler and nebulizer aerosol drug delivery.**
   - **Physics:** Compressible gas + Lagrangian particle tracking, two-phase.
   - **Governing equations:** Continuous-phase Navier–Stokes + discrete particle equations of motion.
   - **CFD Output:** Lung deposition fraction, optimal droplet size.

*(Marks: 1 mark introduction, 4×1.5 marks = 6 marks for the four examples.)*

---

**B(b) Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{998 \times 2.5 \times 0.05}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \dfrac{124.75}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.245 \times 10^5 \approx 1.25 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Determine the flow regime.**

Since $Re = 1.25 \times 10^5 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent** inside the pipe.

**Step 3 — Additional CFD analyses required at the 90° elbow.**

Because of the bend, the following additional physics must be modeled:

- **Secondary (Dean) flow** — Two counter-rotating vortices form in the cross-section due to centrifugal imbalance. The CFD mesh must be **fine enough near the outer wall** to capture the high curvature.
- **Flow separation and recirculation zone** — At the inner wall of the bend, adverse pressure gradient causes **boundary layer separation**. The $k$–$\epsilon$ model with **enhanced wall treatment** is recommended; SST $k$–$\omega$ is an even better choice.
- **Pressure loss coefficient ($K$-factor)** — The CFD must be run over a domain extending at least **10D upstream and 10D downstream** of the bend to capture re-attached flow. Output: bend loss coefficient $K \approx 0.3$–$0.9$ for a 90° standard elbow.
- **Erosion and cavitation risk** — At the outer wall, low local pressure may cause **cavitation**; if so, a multiphase model (e.g., Rayleigh–Plesset + VOF) must be added.
- **Mesh refinement at the bend** — Local mesh size must be at least 5–10 times finer than the straight-pipe mesh to resolve the curvature-driven secondary flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Trap):** Students often use $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ with the wrong diameter (forgetting it is a circular pipe, not a duct), or report "laminar" because they compare with air's $Re_{crit} = 2300$. Water pipe critical $Re$ is the same **4000** value — students must state **why** the flow is turbulent and **what additional physics** the bend introduces. Marks lost: **2/7** for wrong conclusion on regime, **2/7** for not naming Dean vortices.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-second revision before entering the KTU exam hall. Memorize these bullet points.**

- ✅ **Definition:** CFD = numerical solution of NS + continuity + energy equations on a discrete mesh.
- ✅ **6-Step Workflow:** CAD → Mesh → Physics/BCs → Solve → Post-Process → Validate.
- ✅ **Top 5 application domains:** Aerospace, Automotive, Turbomachinery, Biomedical, Electronics Cooling.
- ✅ **Most-used non-dimensional numbers:** $Re$ (laminar/turbulent), $Ma$ (compressible/incompressible), $Pr$ (thermal), $Nu$ (convective heat transfer), $C_D$ and $C_L$ (aerodynamic performance).
- ✅ **Critical $Re$ values to memorize:** $Re < 2300$ → laminar pipe flow; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent pipe flow; $Re > 5 \times 10^5$ → turbulent external flow.
- ✅ **Critical $Ma$ values:** $Ma < 0.3$ → incompressible; $0.3 < Ma < 0.8$ → subsonic compressible; $0.8 < Ma < 1.2$ → transonic (shocks!); $Ma > 5$ → hypersonic.
- ✅ **Continuity equation (incompressible):** $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$ → $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y + \partial w/\partial z = 0$.
- ✅ **Continuity equation (compressible):** $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}) = 0$.
- ✅ **RANS equation:** $\rho D\bar{u}_i/Dt = -\partial \bar{p}/\partial x_i + \mu \partial^2 \bar{u}_i/\partial x_j^2 + \partial \tau_{ij}^R / \partial x_j$ — extra Reynolds stress terms require **closure** (turbulence model).
- ✅ **Turbulence model quick-pick:** Aerospace → SST k–ω; Industrial/ventilation → Realizable k–ε; High-Reynolds external → k–ε; Wall-bounded low-Re → k–ω.
- ✅ **CFD strength over experiments:** Parameter sweeps, full-field data, no sensor intrusion, dangerous flows (nuclear, re-entry), low cost per design iteration.
- ✅ **CFD limitation:** Turbulence model uncertainty, mesh-dependence, computational cost for high-Re industrial flows — always validate against experiment!
- ✅ **Standard atmospheric constants to remember:** $\rho_{SL} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/(m·s), $a_{15°C} = 340.3$ m/s.
- ✅ **KPI definitions to write verbatim:** $C_D = 2F_D / (\rho V^2 A)$, $C_L = 2F_L / (\rho V^2 A)$, $Nu = hL/k$.
- ✅ **Combustion chamber mass balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ — the simplest CFD-derived equation; frequently tested.
- ✅ **Examiner's golden rule for KTU Part B answers:** Always *begin with a definition*, *show the governing equations*, *justify any simplifying assumption*, and *end with the engineering significance*. A 7-mark answer that lacks any of these four components will lose 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discretization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT415 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discretization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Discretization** is the foundational numerical procedure in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) by which a continuous mathematical model — typically expressed in the form of Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) governing fluid flow, heat transfer, and mass transport — is converted into a system of algebraic equations solvable on a digital computer. This is achieved by replacing the continuous spatial and temporal domain with a finite set of discrete points, lines, surfaces, or volumes (a **computational grid** or **mesh**), and approximating the continuous derivatives and integrals in the governing equations using algebraic expressions evaluated at these discrete locations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT415 – Module 2):** Discretization is the *heart* of the CFD solution process. The three principal discretization philosophies that you must master are:
> 1. **Finite Difference Method (FDM)**
> 2. **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**
> 3. **Finite Element Method (FEM)**
>
> A common board exam question in the previous years has been: *"Compare the three discretization approaches."* Be ready!

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are describing the shape of a smooth, curved coastline to a friend who has never seen it. Instead of giving them a continuous, infinitely detailed description, you pick **a fixed set of landmark points** along the coast (a lighthouse, a pier, a rock) and tell them the exact height of the tide at *only* those points. By joining these points with straight lines, you get a **piecewise approximation** of the real coast.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Interpretation:**
> - The **continuous coastline** = the *true* physical flow field (velocity, pressure, temperature at *every* point in space and time).
> - The **landmark points** = the *discrete grid nodes* used in CFD.
> - The **piecewise straight lines** = the *algebraic approximations* (difference formulas) of derivatives.
> - The **error in the shape** = the **truncation error** of the discretization scheme.

The finer the landmarks you pick (i.e., the smaller $\Delta x$), the closer your piecewise description matches the real coast — and the more accurate (but more computationally expensive) the simulation becomes.

## 1.3 Why Discretization is Necessary

A real-world fluid-flow problem is described by the **Navier–Stokes equations** — a coupled, non-linear system of partial differential equations. For all but the most trivial geometries, these equations have **no closed-form analytical solution**. Digital computers, however, can only:
- Store information at a **finite** number of locations (memory cells).
- Perform a **finite** number of arithmetic operations per unit time.

Therefore, the infinite-information continuous problem must be **reduced** to a finite problem. That reduction process is precisely what *discretization* accomplishes.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Parameters Affected by Discretization:**
> - **Spatial step ($\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, $\Delta z$)** — controls geometric resolution.
> - **Time step ($\Delta t$)** — controls temporal resolution.
> - **Grid quality (skewness, aspect ratio, orthogonality)** — controls solution fidelity.
> - **Total Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — equal to (Number of nodes) × (Number of variables).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Discretization

### 2.1.1 Finite Difference Method (FDM)
- **Origin:** The oldest and most intuitive method; rooted in Taylor-series expansions.
- **Approach:** Approximates derivatives in the governing PDE directly by replacing them with **finite difference** formulas at each grid node.
- **Best suited for:** Simple, regular, structured grids (rectangular domains).
- **Mathematical basis:** Taylor series, polynomial interpolation.

### 2.1.2 Finite Volume Method (FVM)
- **Origin:** The workhorse of *industrial* CFD codes (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, STAR-CD).
- **Approach:** The computational domain is subdivided into a finite number of small **control volumes (cells)**. The governing PDE is integrated over each cell, and the divergence theorem is used to convert volume integrals of gradients into surface integrals of fluxes. This yields algebraic equations that **conserve mass, momentum, and energy exactly** for each cell.
- **Best suited for:** Complex, irregular geometries with unstructured grids.
- **Mathematical basis:** Conservation principles + divergence theorem.

### 2.1.3 Finite Element Method (FEM)
- **Origin:** Borrowed from structural mechanics, widely used in solid mechanics and heat transfer.
- **Approach:** The domain is divided into small **elements** (triangles, quadrilaterals, tetrahedra). The solution is approximated as a weighted sum of simple **basis (shape) functions** defined on each element. A variational or weighted-residual formulation (e.g., Galerkin method) is used to derive the algebraic system.
- **Best suited for:** Irregular domains, problems requiring rigorous mathematical analysis.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Discretization Process

1. **Governing Equation Selection:** Identify the relevant PDE (Laplace, Poisson, Convection-Diffusion, Navier–Stokes).
2. **Domain Definition:** Define the continuous physical region $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ bounded by $\partial \Omega$.
3. **Grid / Mesh Generation:** Replace $\Omega$ with a discrete set of points, cells, or elements. Define a node-indexing scheme $i, j, k$.
4. **Discretization of Derivatives:** Replace continuous derivatives $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ with finite-difference / finite-volume / finite-element expressions.
5. **Discretization of the Domain:** Replace integrals (e.g., $\int \phi \, dV$) with numerical quadrature (trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule, midpoint rule).
6. **Assembly of Algebraic System:** A system of the form $[A]\{\phi\} = \{b\}$ is produced.
7. **Boundary Condition Application:** Dirichlet, Neumann, and Robin conditions are imposed at the discrete boundary nodes.
8. **Solution & Post-Processing:** Solve the linear / non-linear system, then compute derived quantities (streamlines, vorticity, shear stress).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three methods? (The 'How' behind the 'Why')**
> All three methods converge to the *same* physical solution as the grid is refined. They differ in:
> - The **starting point** (pointwise, integral, variational).
> - The **ease of grid generation** for complex geometries.
> - The **conservation properties** (FVM is strictly conservative; FDM may or may not be).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Discrete Approximation | Formula | Truncation Error (Leading Term) | Order of Accuracy |
|---|------------------------|---------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Forward Difference** for $\partial \phi / \partial x$ | $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x}$ | $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2}\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\big\vert_i$ | $O(\Delta x)$ — First-order |
| 2 | **Backward Difference** for $\partial \phi / \partial x$ | $\dfrac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $+\dfrac{\Delta x}{2}\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\big\vert_i$ | $O(\Delta x)$ — First-order |
| 3 | **Central Difference** for $\partial \phi / \partial x$ | $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{6}\dfrac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\big\vert_i$ | $O(\Delta x)^2$ — Second-order |
| 4 | **Central Difference** for $\partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2$ | $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2}$ | $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{12}\dfrac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\big\vert_i$ | $O(\Delta x)^2$ — Second-order |
| 5 | **Forward Time, Central Space (FTCS)** for $\partial \phi / \partial t$ | $\dfrac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t}$ | $O(\Delta t)$ | First-order in time |
| 6 | **Crank–Nicolson** for $\partial \phi / \partial t$ | $\dfrac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left( F_i^{n+1} + F_i^n \right)$ | $O(\Delta t)^2$ | Second-order in time |
| 7 | **General 1-D Convection–Diffusion (steady)** | $u\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \nu \dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}$ | Depends on scheme | — |
| 8 | **Truncation Error (TE)** | $TE = \phi_{\text{exact}} - \phi_{\text{discrete}}$ | — | — |
| 9 | **Richardson Extrapolation** | $\phi_{\text{improved}} = \dfrac{4\phi_{h/2} - \phi_h}{3}$ | $O(\Delta x)^4$ | Boosts order by 2 |
| 10 | **Grid Independence Criterion** | $\vert \phi_{h} - \phi_{h/2} \vert \le \epsilon_{\text{tol}}$ | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Formatting Trap:** In markdown tables, never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` for absolute value. Always write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $x \mid$ to keep the table structure intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Discretization of the Navier–Stokes equations over an aircraft wing to predict lift and drag without wind-tunnel testing.
- **Automotive:** Predicting in-cylinder combustion and pollutant formation in IC engines.
- **Biomedical:** Modelling blood flow through stenosed arteries (used in designing stents).
- **Civil Engineering:** Dam spillway hydraulics, wind loading on skyscrapers, pollutant dispersion in urban canyons.
- **Electronics Cooling:** Thermal management of CPU/GPU heat sinks using conjugate heat-transfer discretization.
- **Weather & Climate:** Global atmospheric models discretize the primitive equations on a 3-D spherical grid.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** A function $\phi(x, y, t)$ evaluated at node $i$ along $x$, $j$ along $y$, and time-level $n$ is written as $\phi_{i,j}^n$. The uniform grid spacings are $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, and the time step is $\Delta t$.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward-Difference Approximation (1st Order)

We start from the **Taylor series expansion** of $\phi$ about the node $i$, evaluated one node to the right ($x_i + \Delta x$):

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} \left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} \left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!} \left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i + \cdots
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for the first derivative.** Rearranging to isolate $\left.\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i$:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i - \cdots
$$

**Step 2 — Truncate and define the discrete operator.** Dropping all terms of order $(\Delta x)^2$ and higher gives the **forward-difference** formula:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error} \quad \tau_f = -\frac{\Delta x}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i + O(\Delta x)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the order.** The leading error term is proportional to $\Delta x$, so the approximation is **first-order accurate**: $O(\Delta x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** A first-order scheme is *cheap* (only one extra node needed) but *inaccurate* on coarse grids. Industry simulations therefore prefer second-order central differences wherever stability permits.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Backward-Difference Approximation (1st Order)

The Taylor series expansion one node to the *left* of $i$ is:

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} \left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} \left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \cdots
$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange:**

$$
\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i = \frac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \cdots
$$

**Step 2 — Truncate:**

$$
\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i \approx \frac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x}, \qquad \tau_b = \frac{\Delta x}{2}\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i + O(\Delta x)^2
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Central-Difference Approximation (2nd Order)

**Step 1 — Add the forward and backward Taylor series expansions:**

$$
\phi_{i+1} + \phi_{i-1} = 2\phi_i + (\Delta x)^2 \left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{12}\left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i + \cdots
$$

(The odd-derivative terms cancel out — this is the *key* trick.)

**Step 2 — Solve for the first derivative by *subtracting* the two expansions:**

$$
\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1} = 2\Delta x \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i + \frac{2(\Delta x)^3}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \frac{2(\Delta x)^5}{120}\left.\frac{\partial^5 \phi}{\partial x^5}\right|_i + \cdots
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i - O(\Delta x)^4
$$

**Step 3 — Truncate:**

$$
\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}, \qquad \tau_c = -\frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + O(\Delta x)^4
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** The central difference is **second-order accurate** because the leading error is $O(\Delta x)^2$. It is the default spatial discretization in most CFD codes for diffusion-dominated problems.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Central-Difference Second Derivative (2nd Order)

Re-arrange the *sum* of the Taylor series from Step 1 of §3.3:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{12}\left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i - O(\Delta x)^4
$$

**Discrete operator:**

$$
\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2}, \qquad \tau = O(\Delta x)^2
$$

## 3.5 Application: Discretization of the 1-D Steady Heat Equation

The continuous governing equation (Poisson's equation in 1-D) is:

$$
\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Apply central-difference at node $i$:**

$$
\frac{T_{i+1} - 2T_i + T_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply through and obtain the algebraic stencil:**

$$
T_{i-1} - 2T_i + T_{i+1} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Write the system for an $N$-node interior.** For $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$, and given $T_0$ and $T_{N+1}$ (boundary values), the global matrix system is $[A]\{T\} = \{b\}$, where $[A]$ is a **tridiagonal matrix** of the form:

$$
[A] = \frac{1}{(\Delta x)^2}\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & -2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve (e.g., by Thomas algorithm for tridiagonal systems)**, and the exact solution $T(x) = T_0 + (T_{N+1} - T_0) \dfrac{x}{L}$ is recovered exactly (since the second derivative is identically zero, the truncation error vanishes — the scheme is *exact* for linear profiles).

## 3.6 Discretization of the 1-D Unsteady Convection–Diffusion Equation (Explicit FTCS)

The governing PDE is:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply forward difference in time, central differences in space:**

$$
\frac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t} + u \frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n}{2\Delta x} = \nu \frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n}{(\Delta x)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve explicitly for the new time level $\phi_i^{n+1}$:**

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n - \frac{u \Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n\right) + \frac{\nu \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define dimensionless numbers:**

$$
r = \frac{\nu \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2} \quad \text{(diffusion number)}, \qquad c = \frac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x} \quad \text{(Courant number)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compact form:**

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n - \frac{c}{2}\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n\right) + r\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Stability bound (CFL for diffusion):** For the explicit FTCS scheme, stability requires $r \le \frac{1}{2}$ and $\lvert c \rvert \le 1$ (depending on the formulation). This is the famous **von Neumann stability constraint**.

## 3.7 Order of Accuracy from Truncation Error

If a discrete operator $\mathcal{L}_h$ acting on the exact solution $\phi_{\text{exact}}$ produces:

$$
\mathcal{L}_h \phi_{\text{exact}} = \mathcal{L} \phi_{\text{exact}} + \tau_h
$$

where $\tau_h = O(\Delta x)^p$, then the scheme is said to be **$p$-th order accurate** and **consistent** with the continuous operator $\mathcal{L}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lax's Equivalence Theorem:** For a *linear* PDE, **consistency + stability $\Rightarrow$ convergence**, and the order of accuracy is preserved in the limit of grid refinement.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Discretization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous PDE Formulation] --> B[Domain Definition]
    B --> C[Grid Generation Module]
    C --> D[Node Indexing i j k]
    D --> E{Discretization Method}
    E --> F1[Finite Difference Method FDM]
    E --> F2[Finite Volume Method FVM]
    E --> F3[Finite Element Method FEM]
    F1 --> G1[Replace Derivatives with Difference Formulas]
    F2 --> G2[Integrate PDE over Control Volumes]
    F3 --> G3[Weighted Residual or Variational Form]
    G1 --> H[Assembly of Algebraic System A x equals b]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    I --> J[Solve Linear or Non Linear System]
    J --> K[Post Processing Visualization]
    K --> L[Grid Independence Check]
    L --> M{Converged Solution}

    M -- No --> C
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**What is meant by discretization in CFD? List the three major discretization methods.**

**Model Answer:**
Discretization is the process of converting the continuous governing equations of a fluid flow problem (PDEs) into a set of discrete algebraic equations that can be solved on a digital computer. This is done by replacing the continuous domain with a finite number of grid points, control volumes, or elements, and approximating the continuous derivatives using algebraic difference formulas.

The three major discretization methods are:
1. **Finite Difference Method (FDM)**
2. **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**
3. **Finite Element Method (FEM)** **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define truncation error. What is the order of accuracy of the central difference scheme for the first derivative?**

**Model Answer:**
The **truncation error** is the difference between the exact value of a derivative (as obtained from a Taylor series expansion) and its discrete finite-difference approximation. It represents the error introduced by replacing the continuous differential operator with a discrete algebraic operator.

For the central difference scheme:
$$
\left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \cdots
$$
Since the leading error term is proportional to $(\Delta x)^2$, the central difference scheme is **second-order accurate**, i.e., $O(\Delta x)^2$. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign errors** are extremely common when handling the Taylor series — students forget alternating signs on odd powers of $\Delta x$. Always double-check signs against a published derivation.
> 2. **Confusing truncation error with discretization error** — TE is *local*, while the global discretization error also includes contributions from boundary condition imposition and round-off.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the order of accuracy explicitly** — examiners deduct 1 mark if the $O(\Delta x)$ qualifier is missing.
> 4. **Mixing up $O(\Delta x)$ and $O(\Delta x)^2$** in central-difference derivations — these are NOT the same; the central difference of the *first* derivative is $O(\Delta x)^2$ only when the function is sufficiently smooth.
> 5. **Not writing the discrete system in matrix form** for the 1-D heat equation — examiners often expect a clear $[A]\{T\}=\{b\}$ representation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the second-order central-difference approximation for $\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}$ using the Taylor series expansion. Hence obtain the truncation error. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Starting from the Taylor series expansions of $\phi$ about node $i$:

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!}\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!}\left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i + \cdots
$$

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!}\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!}\left.\frac{\partial^3 \phi}{\partial x^3}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!}\left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i - \cdots
$$

**Adding** the two expansions, the odd-derivative terms cancel:

$$
\phi_{i+1} + \phi_{i-1} = 2\phi_i + (\Delta x)^2 \left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{12}\left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i + O(\Delta x)^6
$$

**Solving** for the second derivative:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{12}\left.\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i - O(\Delta x)^4
$$

**Discrete formula:** $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2}$

**Truncation error:** $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{12}\left.\dfrac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4}\right|_i + O(\Delta x)^4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\;$ **Second-order accurate**, $O(\Delta x)^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Using the second-order central-difference scheme, discretize the 1-D steady heat conduction equation $\dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$ for a rod of length $L$ with $N+1$ interior nodes. Write the resulting system in matrix form and explain the boundary-condition treatment. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Discretize the PDE at node $i$:**

$$
\frac{T_{i+1} - 2T_i + T_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_{i-1} - 2T_i + T_{i+1} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Write for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$:**
- $i=1$: $T_0 - 2T_1 + T_2 = 0$
- $i=2$: $T_1 - 2T_2 + T_3 = 0$
- $\vdots$
- $i=N$: $T_{N-1} - 2T_N + T_{N+1} = 0$

**Step 3 — Move the known boundary values $T_0$ and $T_{N+1}$ to the right-hand side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2T_1 + T_2 &= -T_0 \\
T_1 - 2T_2 + T_3 &= 0 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
T_{N-1} - 2T_N &= -T_{N+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Matrix form $[A]\{T\} = \{b\}$:**

$$
\frac{1}{(\Delta x)^2}\begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ \vdots \\ T_N \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -T_0/(\Delta x)^2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ -T_{N+1}/(\Delta x)^2 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Boundary condition treatment:**
- **Dirichlet (specified temperature):** $T_0$ and $T_{N+1}$ are moved to the RHS. The matrix is *tridiagonal* and symmetric positive-definite.
- **Neumann (specified heat flux):** A ghost node is introduced and a finite-difference expression for $\partial T / \partial x$ at the boundary is used.
- **Convection (Robin):** A combination of $T$ and $\partial T / \partial x$ is specified; the boundary equation becomes a linear relation between the ghost node and the interior node.

The system is then solved using the **Thomas algorithm** (an $O(N)$ solver for tridiagonal systems). For the case $T_0 = T_L$ and $T_{N+1} = T_R$, the exact solution is $T(x_i) = T_L + (T_R - T_L) x_i / L$, which the second-order scheme reproduces *exactly* because the truncation error vanishes for a linear profile. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Compare the Finite Difference Method (FDM), Finite Volume Method (FVM), and Finite Element Method (FEM) along the following dimensions: (i) mathematical starting point, (ii) conservation property, (iii) grid type suitability, (iv) computational cost, and (v) typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Dimension | FDM | FVM | FEM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| (i) Mathematical starting point | Taylor series expansion of derivatives | Integral form of governing equation over control volumes; divergence theorem | Variational / weighted residual formulation with shape functions |
| (ii) Conservation property | Not inherently conservative; may be conservative only on special grids | Strictly conservative — flux leaving one cell exactly equals flux entering the neighbour | Conservative only with careful formulation (e.g., mixed FEM) |
| (iii) Grid type suitability | Best for structured, regular grids (rectangular domains) | Excellent on structured, unstructured, and hybrid grids | Excellent on irregular, unstructured grids |
| (iv) Computational cost | Cheapest for simple problems (direct stencil evaluation) | Moderate; flux calculations add overhead but enable complex geometries | Most expensive due to large sparse matrices and iterative solvers |
| (v) Industrial application | Educational codes, simple 1-D/2-D thermal analysis | Industry-standard CFD (Fluent, OpenFOAM, CFX) for aerodynamics, turbomachinery | Solid mechanics, structural analysis, electromagnetics, complex thermal stress |

**Justification (each line = 1 mark, total 7):** The FDM is mathematically the simplest because it operates pointwise on Taylor series, but its strict requirement of structured grids limits geometric flexibility. The FVM is the **workhorse of industrial CFD** because conservation of mass, momentum, and energy at the discrete level is guaranteed by construction — a critical requirement when solving the Navier–Stokes equations over complex engine or aircraft geometries. The FEM, while computationally heavier, is preferred when mathematically rigorous error estimates are required (e.g., in $h$- and $p$-refinement strategies) or when the geometry is highly irregular. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the explicit FTCS (Forward Time, Central Space) discretization of the 1-D unsteady convection–diffusion equation $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \nu\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}$. Identify the dimensionless stability parameters and quote the corresponding CFL stability bound. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forward difference in time:**

$$
\frac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t} = \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\right|_i^n + O(\Delta t)
$$

**Step 2 — Central difference in space (convection):**

$$
\frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n}{2\Delta x} = \left.\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right|_i^n + O(\Delta x)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Central difference in space (diffusion):**

$$
\frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n}{(\Delta x)^2} = \left.\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right|_i^n + O(\Delta x)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the PDE:**

$$
\frac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t} + u\frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n}{2\Delta x} = \nu\frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n}{(\Delta x)^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the next time level (explicit scheme):**

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n - \frac{u \Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n\right) + \frac{\nu \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Define dimensionless numbers:**

$$
c = \frac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x} \quad \text{(Courant number — controls convection stability)}
$$

$$
r = \frac{\nu \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2} \quad \text{(diffusion number — grid Fourier number)}
$$

**Step 7 — Compact form:**

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n - \frac{c}{2}\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n\right) + r\left(\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Stability bounds (from von Neumann analysis):**
- For pure **diffusion** (i.e., $u = 0$): the explicit scheme is stable iff $r \le \tfrac{1}{2}$.
- For pure **convection** (i.e., $\nu = 0$): the central-space scheme is *unconditionally unstable* in time; an *upwind* spatial discretization must be used, with the bound $\lvert c \rvert \le 1$ (CFL condition).
- For the **coupled** convection–diffusion problem, the combined constraint becomes $\lvert c \rvert + 2r \le 1$ (in the simplest case).

The stability constraint is often expressed as a CFL-type bound $\Delta t \le \dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{2\nu + u \Delta x}$ for safety. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield KTU Board Points):**
>
> - **Discretization** = converting continuous PDEs into discrete algebraic equations on a finite grid.
> - Three main methods: **FDM, FVM, FEM** — know the comparison table cold.
> - **FDM** is based on **Taylor series**; suited to structured grids; not inherently conservative.
> - **FVM** is based on **conservation + divergence theorem**; conserves mass, momentum, energy at cell level; preferred for industrial CFD.
> - **FEM** is based on **variational / weighted-residual** formulation using shape functions; best for irregular geometries and rigorous error analysis.
> - **Forward difference:** $O(\Delta x)$ — uses $(i+1, i)$.
> - **Backward difference:** $O(\Delta x)$ — uses $(i, i-1)$.
> - **Central difference:** $O(\Delta x)^2$ — uses $(i+1, i-1)$; preferred for smooth fields.
> - **Second derivative central difference:** $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2}$ — second-order accurate.
> - **Truncation Error (TE):** difference between the exact derivative and its finite-difference approximation; derived by Taylor series.
> - **Consistency:** a scheme is consistent if TE $\to 0$ as $\Delta x \to 0$.
> - **Stability:** a scheme is stable if round-off errors do not grow unboundedly as the solution progresses.
> - **Convergence:** a scheme converges if the numerical solution $\to$ exact solution as $\Delta x \to 0$.
> - **Lax's Equivalence Theorem:** for a *linear* PDE, consistency + stability $\Rightarrow$ convergence.
> - **FTCS explicit scheme** for unsteady problems: $\phi_i^{n+1}$ is computed directly from known $n$-level values.
> - **Courant number $c = u \Delta t / \Delta x$** and **diffusion number $r = \nu \Delta t / (\Delta x)^2$** govern the stability of explicit schemes.
> - **Crank–Nicolson** is the standard second-order-in-time implicit scheme; unconditionally stable for diffusion.
> - Always quote the **order of accuracy** explicitly when writing any difference formula.
> - **Grid independence** must be verified by halving $\Delta x$ and confirming the solution does not change appreciably.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Converting Derivatives to Their Finite Difference Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT415 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Converting Derivatives to Their Finite Difference Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **finite difference method (FDM)**, a continuous differential operator (such as $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ or $\dfrac{d^2 f}{dx^2}$) is **approximated** by an algebraic expression involving the **discrete values** of the function $f$ evaluated at neighbouring grid nodes separated by a finite spacing $\Delta x$. This process is called **converting derivatives to their finite difference forms**.

Mathematically, a derivative at node $i$ is replaced as:

$$\left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\Delta x}\,\mathcal{F}\bigl(f_{i-1},\,f_{i},\,f_{i+1},\,\ldots\bigr)$$

where $\mathcal{F}$ is a **linear combination** of function values and the accuracy of the approximation depends on how many neighbouring nodes are used and how they are weighted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The derivation of all standard finite difference approximations begins from the **Taylor series expansion** of the function about a given node. You are expected to derive forward, backward, and central differences and clearly state the order of accuracy (truncation error) of each.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving along a straight highway and your **speedometer breaks down**. You still want to know your instantaneous speed. The only thing you have is the **odometer**, which gives total distance.

- If you read the odometer now and again **10 seconds later**, you can estimate average speed: $v \approx \dfrac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$.
- The smaller the time gap $\Delta t$, the closer this estimate is to the true instantaneous speed.
- The **direction** of the gap (looking ahead vs looking back) gives different recipes, just as **forward**, **backward**, and **central** differences are different recipes in CFD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A derivative is essentially a *limit* as the spacing $\Delta x \to 0$. A finite difference is the *finite* version of that limit — the spacing is **non-zero** and the answer carries a small **truncation error** that shrinks as $\Delta x$ shrinks.

## 1.3 The Role of Discretization in CFD

The continuous PDE that governs fluid flow (e.g., the Navier–Stokes equation) must be solved at a **finite number of points** on a computational grid. This conversion — from calculus to algebra — is the first step of any FDM-based CFD code. The three pillars of the conversion are:

1. **Grid generation** — placement of nodes $x_i = i\Delta x$.
2. **Derivative approximation** — replacing $\dfrac{d}{dx}$ with algebraic stencils.
3. **Error analysis** — quantifying how close the algebraic form is to the true derivative.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Valuation:** The *order of accuracy* $p$ of a finite difference scheme is defined such that the truncation error behaves as $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^p)$. A scheme with $p = 2$ is called **second-order accurate**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taylor Series Foundation

Every finite difference formula in this module is derived from the **Taylor series expansion** of a smooth function $f(x)$ about a node $x_i$. For a small displacement $\Delta x$:

$$f(x_i + \Delta x) = f(x_i) + \Delta x \frac{df}{dx}\Big|_{i} + \frac{\Delta x^{\,2}}{2!}\frac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}\Big|_{i} + \frac{\Delta x^{\,3}}{3!}\frac{d^{3}f}{dx^{3}}\Big|_{i} + \cdots$$

$$f(x_i - \Delta x) = f(x_i) - \Delta x \frac{df}{dx}\Big|_{i} + \frac{\Delta x^{\,2}}{2!}\frac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}\Big|_{i} - \frac{\Delta x^{\,3}}{3!}\frac{d^{3}f}{dx^{3}}\Big|_{i} + \cdots$$

By **adding, subtracting, or truncating** these series at appropriate terms and solving for the derivative of interest, we obtain the various finite difference forms.

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $f_i \equiv f(x_i)$ is used throughout CFD textbooks to keep equations compact. You are expected to use the same shorthand in KTU examinations.

## 2.2 Classification of Finite Difference Approximations

The classification is based on the **stencil direction** and the **number of nodes** used.

### (A) By Stencil Direction

| Type | Uses Nodes | First Derivative Form | Truncation Error |
|------|------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Forward Difference (F.D.)** | $f_i$ and $f_{i+1}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1}-f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| **Backward Difference (B.D.)** | $f_{i-1}$ and $f_i$ | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| **Central Difference (C.D.)** | $f_{i-1}$ and $f_{i+1}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1}-f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ |

### (B) By Order of Accuracy

| Scheme | First Derivative Formula | Order |
|--------|------------------------|-------|
| 1st-order forward | $\dfrac{f_{i+1}-f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| 1st-order backward | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| 2nd-order central | $\dfrac{f_{i+1}-f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ |
| 2nd-order forward (3-point) | $\dfrac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ |
| 4th-order central (5-point) | $\dfrac{-f_{i+2} + 8f_{i+1} - 8f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{12\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{4})$ |

### (C) Second Derivative Approximations

| Scheme | Formula | Order |
|--------|---------|-------|
| 2nd-order central (2nd derivative) | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^{2}}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ |
| 4th-order central (2nd derivative) | $\dfrac{-f_{i+2} + 16f_{i+1} - 30f_i + 16f_{i-1} - f_{i-2}}{12\Delta x^{2}}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{4})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For the **second derivative**, both forward and backward 2-point stencils have truncation error of order $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$, but they are **not used** in practice because the central 3-point stencil already gives **second-order accuracy** with the same number of nodes. Examiners frequently test whether you know this.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Approximations

- **Aerospace Engineering:** Computing wall shear stress $\tau_w = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}\big|_{wall}$ in boundary layers.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Solving 1-D heat conduction $\dfrac{d^{2}T}{dx^{2}} = 0$ across a fin.
- **Civil Engineering:** Finite-difference solution of Laplace's equation $\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$ for dam seepage and torsion problems.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Modeling plug-flow reactors with axial diffusion term $D\dfrac{d^{2}C}{dx^{2}}$.

## 2.4 Logical Procedure for Derivation (Mental Framework)

1. **Write the Taylor series** of $f$ at $x_{i+1}$ (or $x_{i-1}$, or both) about $x_i$.
2. **Rearrange** to isolate the derivative of interest on the left-hand side.
3. **Truncate** all terms beyond a chosen order (these become the truncation error).
4. **Replace** the continuous function values by their discrete nodal samples $f_{i-1}, f_i, f_{i+1}, \ldots$
5. **Identify** the leading dropped term and write the scheme's order of accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation of Forward Difference for the First Derivative

**Step 1 — Write the Taylor expansion of $f$ at $x_{i+1}$:**

$$f(x_{i+1}) = f(x_i) + \Delta x \frac{df}{dx}\Big|_{i} + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2!}\frac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}\Big|_{i} + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{3!}\frac{d^{3}f}{dx^{3}}\Big|_{i} + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{4!}\frac{d^{4}f}{dx^{4}}\Big|_{i} + \cdots$$

In shorthand (using $f_{i+1} = f(x_{i+1})$):

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the first derivative $f'_i$ on the left:**

$$f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{6} f'''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Truncate after the first term on the RHS** (all remaining terms are gathered into the truncation error $\tau$):

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the truncation error.** The first dropped term is $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2}f''_i$, so:

$$\tau_{\text{F.D.}} = -\frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}\Big|_{i} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2}) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$$

The forward difference is therefore **first-order accurate**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Backward Difference for the First Derivative

**Step 1 — Write the Taylor expansion of $f$ at $x_{i-1}$:**

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $f'_i$:**

$$f'_i = \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Truncate to get the backward-difference scheme:**

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Truncation error:**

$$\tau_{\text{B.D.}} = +\frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}\Big|_{i} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2}) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The forward and backward difference formulas are mirror images. Both are $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$, but the leading error term has **opposite sign** — a fact that becomes important in stability analysis of explicit time-marching schemes.

## 3.3 Derivation of Central Difference for the First Derivative

**Step 1 — Write expansions at $x_{i+1}$ and $x_{i-1}$ simultaneously:**

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the second equation from the first** (even-order terms cancel):

$$f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2\Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{3} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{5}}{60} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $f'_i$:**

$$f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{6} f'''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{120} f^{(5)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Truncate to obtain the central-difference formula:**

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Truncation error:**

$$\tau_{\text{C.D.}} = -\frac{\Delta x^{2}}{6}\frac{d^{3}f}{dx^{3}}\Big|_{i} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{4}) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$$

The central difference is **second-order accurate** — it is twice as accurate per unit $\Delta x$ as the one-sided formulas.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Central Difference for the Second Derivative

**Step 1 — Add the two Taylor series** (odd-order terms cancel):

$$f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2f_i + \Delta x^{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^{6}}{360} f^{(6)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $f''_i$:**

$$f''_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^{2}} - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{12} f^{(4)}_i - \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{360} f^{(6)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Truncate:**

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Truncation error:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ — this is the famous **3-point Laplacian stencil** used to discretize diffusion terms.

## 3.5 Derivation of Higher-Order Forward Difference (2nd-order, 3-point)

**Step 1 — Write the Taylor expansions at $x_{i+1}$ and $x_{i+2}$:**

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i+2} = f_i + 2\Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{(2\Delta x)^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{(2\Delta x)^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Form the linear combination $-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}$ to cancel the $f''_i$ term:**

$$4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2} - 3f_i = 2\Delta x\, f'_i - \frac{2\Delta x^{3}}{3} f'''_i + \cdots$$

> The $f''$ term cancels because $4\cdot\dfrac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} - 1\cdot 2\Delta x^{2} = 2\Delta x^{2} - 2\Delta x^{2} = 0$. The $f'$ coefficient becomes $4(\Delta x) - 1(2\Delta x) = 2\Delta x$.

**Step 3 — Solve for $f'_i$:**

$$f'_i = \frac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{3} f'''_i + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Truncate:**

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Order of accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common question asks to "derive a second-order accurate forward difference formula." Students must show both Taylor expansions and the **choice of coefficients** that eliminates the $\Delta x^{2}$ (i.e., $f''$) term.

## 3.6 Derivation of 4th-Order Central Difference (5-point)

**Step 1 — Write Taylor series at $x_{i+1}, x_{i-1}, x_{i+2}, x_{i-2}$:**

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^{5}}{120} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \frac{\Delta x^{5}}{120} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i+2} = f_i + 2\Delta x\, f'_i + 2\Delta x^{2} f''_i + \frac{4\Delta x^{3}}{3} f'''_i + \frac{2\Delta x^{4}}{3} f^{(4)}_i + \frac{4\Delta x^{5}}{15} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i-2} = f_i - 2\Delta x\, f'_i + 2\Delta x^{2} f''_i - \frac{4\Delta x^{3}}{3} f'''_i + \frac{2\Delta x^{4}}{3} f^{(4)}_i - \frac{4\Delta x^{5}}{15} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Form the linear combination $a(f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}) + b(f_{i+2} - f_{i-2})$** and require:

- Coefficient of $f'_i$ to be **1**.
- Coefficient of $f'''_i$ to be **0** (kills the $\Delta x^{3}$ error).
- Coefficient of $f^{(5)}_i$ to be **0** (kills the $\Delta x^{5}$ error).

This yields the system:

$$2a\Delta x + 4b\Delta x = 1$$

$$\frac{a}{3} + \frac{8b}{3} = 0$$

Solving: $a = \dfrac{2}{3\Delta x}, \; b = -\dfrac{1}{12\Delta x}$. Multiplying by $12\Delta x$:

$$8(f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}) - (f_{i+2} - f_{i-2}) = 12\Delta x\, f'_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{5})$$

**Step 3 — Final 4th-order central formula:**

$$\boxed{\;\left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{i} \;\approx\; \frac{-f_{i+2} + 8f_{i+1} - 8f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{12\Delta x}\;}$$

with truncation error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{4})$.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Results

| Derivative | Scheme | Stencil | Order | Error Term |
|------------|--------|---------|-------|------------|
| $f'$ | Forward | $[i, i+1]$ | 1 | $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2}f''_i$ |
| $f'$ | Backward | $[i-1, i]$ | 1 | $+\dfrac{\Delta x}{2}f''_i$ |
| $f'$ | Central | $[i-1, i, i+1]$ | 2 | $-\dfrac{\Delta x^{2}}{6}f'''_i$ |
| $f'$ | Forward 3-pt | $[i, i+1, i+2]$ | 2 | $+\dfrac{\Delta x^{2}}{3}f'''_i$ |
| $f'$ | Central 5-pt | $[i-2, i-1, i+1, i+2]$ | 4 | $+\dfrac{\Delta x^{4}}{30}f^{(5)}_i$ |
| $f''$ | Central 3-pt | $[i-1, i, i+1]$ | 2 | $-\dfrac{\Delta x^{2}}{12}f^{(4)}_i$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Methodology Flowchart — How a Finite Difference Formula is Born

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Need derivative of f at node i] --> B[Write Taylor series about node i at x sub i+1 and x sub i-1]
    B --> C[Choose stencil direction: FORWARD, BACKWARD, or CENTRAL]
    C --> D{One-sided or Two-sided stencil?}
    D -->|One-sided| E[Select number of nodes: 2-point or 3-point]
    D -->|Two-sided| F[Select number of nodes: 3-point or 5-point]
    E --> G[Form linear combination to isolate f prime]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Solve the algebraic system for coefficients]
    H --> I[Truncate higher-order terms]
    I --> J[Write finite difference formula in box]
    J --> K[Identify first dropped term as truncation error]
    K --> L[State order of accuracy: O of Delta x to the p]
    L --> M[END: Scheme ready for use in grid]

    stepA([Begin Derivation]) --> A
    M --> stepZ([Scheme Complete])
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Finite Difference Approximations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Finite Difference Approximations] --> first[First Derivative dF over dx]
    root --> second[Second Derivative d2F over dx2]
    root --> higher[Higher Derivatives d3F, d4F, etc.]

    first --> oneSided[One-Sided Stencils]
    first --> central[Two-Sided Central Stencils]

    oneSided --> fwd[Forward Difference]
    oneSided --> bwd[Backward Difference]
    fwd --> fwd1[2-point: Order 1]
    fwd --> fwd2[3-point: Order 2]
    bwd --> bwd1[2-point: Order 1]
    bwd --> bwd2[3-point: Order 2]

    central --> cen1[3-point: Order 2]
    central --> cen2[5-point: Order 4]
    central --> cen3[7-point: Order 6]

    second --> cen2nd[3-point central: Order 2]
    second --> cen2nd4[5-point central: Order 4]
```

## 4.3 Stencil Visualization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph stencilFWD [Forward 2-pt stencil at node i]
        n1A[node i: f of i] -->|Delta x| n2A[node i+1: f of i+1]
    end

    subgraph stencilBWD [Backward 2-pt stencil at node i]
        n1B[node i-1: f of i-1] -->|Delta x| n2B[node i: f of i]
    end

    subgraph stencilCD [Central 3-pt stencil at node i]
        n1C[node i-1: f of i-1] -->|Delta x| n2C[node i: f of i]
        n2C -->|Delta x| n3C[node i+1: f of i+1]
    end

    subgraph stencilCD5 [Central 5-pt stencil at node i]
        a1[node i-2] -->|2 Delta x| a2[node i-1]
        a2 -->|Delta x| a3[node i]
        a3 -->|Delta x| a4[node i+1]
        a4 -->|2 Delta x| a5[node i+2]
    end
```

## 4.4 Order-of-Accuracy Hierarchy (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Truncation Error Hierarchy] --> L1[Order 1: Linear in Delta x]
    A0 --> L2[Order 2: Quadratic in Delta x]
    A0 --> L4[Order 4: Quartic in Delta x]

    L1 --> E1[Forward 2-pt]
    L1 --> E2[Backward 2-pt]

    L2 --> E3[Central 3-pt first derivative]
    L2 --> E4[Central 3-pt second derivative]
    L2 --> E5[Forward 3-pt]
    L2 --> E6[Backward 3-pt]

    L4 --> E7[Central 5-pt first derivative]
    L4 --> E8[Central 5-pt second derivative]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide:** Each block represents a *family* of stencils with a specific accuracy order. KTU questions often test which family a given coefficient set belongs to.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**State the Taylor series expansion used to derive finite difference approximations and explain its role in the finite difference method.**

**Model Answer:**

The Taylor series of a smooth function $f(x)$ about a node $x_i$ with spacing $\Delta x$ is:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2!} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{3!} f'''_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2!} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{3!} f'''_i + \cdots$$

**Role:** The Taylor series is the **mathematical bridge** between the continuous derivative and its discrete approximation. By truncating the series after a chosen number of terms and rearranging for the desired derivative, we obtain a finite difference formula. The **first omitted term** determines the **order of accuracy** of the resulting scheme.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between forward, backward, and central difference approximations of $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ in terms of accuracy and stencil.**

**Model Answer:**

| Scheme | Stencil | Formula | Order |
|--------|---------|---------|-------|
| Forward | $f_i, f_{i+1}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| Backward | $f_{i-1}, f_i$ | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| Central | $f_{i-1}, f_{i+1}$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ |

The **central difference** uses nodes on both sides of $x_i$ and is **second-order accurate**, while the one-sided differences use nodes on one side only and are **first-order accurate**. For the same $\Delta x$, the central scheme has a smaller truncation error.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **forward difference approximation** of the first derivative $\dfrac{df}{dx}$ using the Taylor series expansion. State the truncation error and order of accuracy. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the Taylor series, derive the **central difference approximation** for the **second derivative** $\dfrac{d^{2}f}{dx^{2}}$ and show that it is **second-order accurate**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Taylor expansion of $f$ at $x_{i+1}$:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for $f'_i$:

$$f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{6} f'''_i - \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Truncating after the first term:

$$\boxed{\;f'_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}\;}$$

**Step 4:** Truncation error: $\tau = -\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$, hence the scheme is **first-order accurate**, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Taylor expansions at $x_{i+1}$ and $x_{i-1}$:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Adding the two equations (odd-order derivatives cancel):

$$f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2f_i + \Delta x^{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $f''_i$:

$$f''_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^{2}} - \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{12} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 4:** Truncating:

$$\boxed{\;f''_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 5:** Truncation error: $\tau = -\dfrac{\Delta x^{2}}{12} f^{(4)}_i$, so the scheme is **second-order accurate**, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive a **second-order accurate forward difference formula** for the first derivative using three points: $f_i, f_{i+1}, f_{i+2}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **fourth-order central difference formula** for the first derivative using the five points $f_{i-2}, f_{i-1}, f_i, f_{i+1}, f_{i+2}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Write Taylor expansions at $x_{i+1}$ and $x_{i+2}$:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x\, f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^{2}}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{3}}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^{4}}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

$$f_{i+2} = f_i + 2\Delta x\, f'_i + 2\Delta x^{2}\, f''_i + \frac{4\Delta x^{3}}{3} f'''_i + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Form the linear combination $a f_i + b f_{i+1} + c f_{i+2}$ such that the $f''_i$ term vanishes. Setting up:

$$\frac{b}{2}\Delta x^{2} + 2c\Delta x^{2} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad b = -4c$$

And the $f'_i$ coefficient must equal 1:

$$b\,\Delta x + 2c\,\Delta x = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad (b + 2c)\Delta x = 1$$

Taking $c = -1$ gives $b = 4$, then $a = -3$.

**Step 3:** Compute the coefficient of $f'_i$:

$$4(\Delta x) + 2(-1)(\Delta x) = 2\Delta x \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Assemble:

$$-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2} = 2\Delta x\, f'_i - \frac{2\Delta x^{3}}{3} f'''_i + \cdots$$

**Step 5:** Solve and truncate:

$$\boxed{\;f'_i \approx \frac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x}\;}$$

with truncation error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$ — **second-order accurate**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Combine the four Taylor expansions (Section 3.6) into the form:

$$f'_i \approx \frac{a(f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}) + b(f_{i+2} - f_{i-2})}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute to find coefficients by matching $f'_i$, $f'''_i$, $f^{(5)}_i$:

$$2a\Delta x + 4b\Delta x = 1$$

$$\frac{a\Delta x^{3}}{3} + \frac{8b\Delta x^{3}}{3} = 0$$

**Step 3:** From the second equation, $a = -8b$. Substituting:

$$2(-8b)\Delta x + 4b\Delta x = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; -12b\,\Delta x = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; b = -\frac{1}{12\Delta x}$$

Then $a = \dfrac{8}{12\Delta x} = \dfrac{2}{3\Delta x}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$f'_i = \frac{\tfrac{2}{3\Delta x}(f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}) - \tfrac{1}{12\Delta x}(f_{i+2} - f_{i-2})}{1}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $12\Delta x$:

$$\boxed{\;f'_i \approx \frac{-f_{i+2} + 8f_{i+1} - 8f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{12\Delta x}\;}$$

with truncation error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{4})$ — **fourth-order accurate**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show the Taylor expansion step-by-step.** Examiners *require* the full expansion, not just the final formula. Marks are split across (i) writing the series, (ii) isolating the derivative, (iii) truncating, (iv) identifying the truncation error.
> 2. **Misidentifying the order of accuracy.** The order is determined by the **lowest power of $\Delta x$** in the truncation error, not by the number of nodes.
> 3. **Sign errors in backward differences.** The backward difference of $f_{i-1}$ has alternating signs in the Taylor series — students frequently drop the $(-1)^n$ pattern.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention boundary-node applicability.** Central difference **cannot** be applied at the first or last grid node; examiners may deduct for not stating this.
> 5. **Confusing forward and backward notation.** $f_{i+1}$ is *forward* from $i$, $f_{i-1}$ is *backward* from $i$ — getting this wrong flips the sign of the entire scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A finite difference replaces a continuous derivative with an algebraic combination of discrete nodal function values.
- **Foundation:** All finite difference formulas are derived from the **Taylor series expansion** of $f$ about a chosen node.
- **One-sided 2-point formulas** (forward and backward) are **first-order accurate** — error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.
- **Central 3-point formulas** are **second-order accurate** — error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{2})$.
- **Central 5-point formula** is **fourth-order accurate** — error $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{4})$.
- **Second derivative** central stencil is $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^{2}}$.
- **Truncation error** = the first term dropped from the Taylor series after truncation.
- **Order of accuracy** $p$ is read from $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^{p})$ in the truncation error.
- **Central differences cannot be used at boundary nodes** — they require values on both sides of $x_i$.
- Higher-order schemes require **more nodes** and **wider stencils**, increasing computational cost but reducing truncation error.
- In **CFD applications**, the central difference is preferred for spatial discretization of diffusion terms and pressure gradients because of its second-order accuracy and zero leading even-order error.
- Forward and backward differences are commonly used in **time-marching schemes** (e.g., explicit Euler) where the time direction is naturally one-sided.
