<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Taylor's Series Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** The **Taylor's Series Approach** is a mathematical procedure that approximates the value of a function and its derivatives at a point by expanding the function as an infinite sum of terms calculated from the values of the function's derivatives at a single, nearby point. In CFD, it is the foundational tool for deriving finite difference approximations of derivatives at a grid point.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are standing at the edge of a hill (point $x_i$) and want to know what the slope (derivative) of the hill looks like one step ahead of you, at $x_{i+1}$. You don't have a measuring tape, but you know the slope *right where you are*. Taylor's series is like using a "memory of the slope" plus a "memory of how the slope is curving" to predict what lies ahead. The more "memories" (higher-order derivatives) you use, the better your prediction, but you also have to do more arithmetic.

### Mathematical Foundation

Given a smooth, continuous function $u(x)$, the Taylor series expansion about a point $x_i$ with a step size $h$ is:

$$u(x_i + h) = u(x_i) + h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2!} u''(x_i) + \frac{h^3}{3!} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{4!} u^{(4)}(x_i) + \dots$$

And for a backward expansion:

$$u(x_i - h) = u(x_i) - h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2!} u''(x_i) - \frac{h^3}{3!} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{4!} u^{(4)}(x_i) - \dots$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When we *truncate* this infinite series (i.e., stop at a certain term), we introduce a **truncation error**. The error is proportional to the first omitted term. This is the genesis of all finite difference formulas.

---

## 1.2 Polynomial Fitting Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Polynomial Fitting Approach** (also called the *Method of Undetermined Coefficients*) assumes that the function $u(x)$ can be locally approximated by a polynomial of degree $n$ passing through a chosen set of neighboring grid points. The derivative at the desired point is then obtained by analytically differentiating the polynomial and evaluating it at that point.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a smooth curve traced on graph paper. Instead of knowing the exact equation of the curve, you "snap" a flexible plastic ruler (a low-order polynomial) onto 2, 3, or 4 nearby dots. The ruler hugs the curve locally. To find the slope (derivative) at a point, you measure the slope of the *ruler* at that point — a much easier task than finding the slope of the unknown curve.

### Standard Assumption
We assume a local polynomial of the form:

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + \dots + a_n x^n$$

The coefficients $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_n$ are determined by enforcing that the polynomial passes exactly through $n+1$ neighboring grid points. The derivative $u'(x_i)$ is then simply obtained from $a_1 + 2 a_2 x_i + 3 a_3 x_i^2 + \dots$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use this approach?** It is more general than Taylor's series and naturally gives the same results. It also clearly demonstrates *why* a particular choice of neighbor points leads to a particular order of accuracy.

---

## 1.3 Forward, Backward, and Central Differences — First Order Derivatives

These are the three canonical finite difference approximations of $u'(x_i)$:

| Approximation | Neighbor Used | Truncation Error Order | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Difference** | $x_i$ and $x_{i+1}$ | $O(h)$ | Upstream marching schemes |
| **Backward Difference** | $x_{i-1}$ and $x_i$ | $O(h)$ | Downstream marching schemes |
| **Central Difference** | $x_{i-1}$ and $x_{i+1}$ | $O(h^2)$ | Most CFD codes (preferred) |

> [!TIP]
> **CFD Rule of Thumb:** Whenever possible, use **central differences** because their truncation error is $O(h^2)$ — meaning if you halve the grid spacing, the error drops by a factor of 4, not 2.

---

## 1.4 Second-Order Derivative Approximations

The second derivative $u''(x_i)$ appears in every diffusion term (e.g., the viscous term in the Navier-Stokes equations). The standard central approximation has the elegant symmetric form:

$$\frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^2} \approx u''(x_i)$$

This is the famous **three-point stencil** for the Laplacian operator in 1D.

---

## 1.5 Solution of PDEs Using Finite Difference Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** for PDEs converts a continuous Partial Differential Equation into a system of algebraic equations by replacing each spatial derivative with a finite difference approximation on a discrete grid, and each time derivative with a forward or backward difference in time.

The general workflow is:
1. **Discretize the domain** into a grid (mesh) of points $(x_i, y_j, t^n)$.
2. **Approximate derivatives** at each grid point using Taylor series or polynomial fitting.
3. **Assemble the algebraic system** of equations coupling neighboring nodes.
4. **Apply boundary and initial conditions** to close the system.
5. **Solve** the resulting linear/nonlinear system (direct or iterative).

---

## 1.6 Discretization Error, Truncation Error, and Round-Off Error

These are the **three pillars of numerical error** in CFD. Every computed solution is contaminated by them.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Discretization Error** = Difference between the *exact solution of the discrete equations* and the *exact solution of the continuous PDE*. It arises purely from replacing derivatives by finite differences.
> - **Truncation Error** = The error introduced when a Taylor series is *truncated* to form a finite difference formula. It is a *local* property of the formula and is $O(h^n)$ for some $n$.
> - **Round-Off Error** = The error due to the *finite number of digits* a computer can store (e.g., double precision gives ~15–16 significant digits).

### The Trade-off
Reducing $h$ → **decreases** discretization and truncation error, but **increases** round-off error (more arithmetic operations). The optimal $h$ is where the total error is minimized.

---

## 1.7 Consistency, Stability, and Convergence (The CFL Trinity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lax's Equivalence Theorem:** *For a well-posed linear initial value problem and a consistent finite difference scheme, **stability is the necessary and sufficient condition for convergence**.*

| Concept | Definition | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | The truncation error of the FDM → 0 as $h, \Delta t \to 0$ | "Does the discrete equation reduce to the PDE in the limit?" |
| **Stability** | Round-off/initial errors do not grow unboundedly as the iteration proceeds | "Will the algorithm blow up?" |
| **Convergence** | The numerical solution → exact solution as $h, \Delta t \to 0$ | "Will the answer approach the true answer?" |

---

## 1.8 Iterative Convergence, Rate of Convergence, and Termination

For large sparse systems (typical in CFD), iterative solvers (Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, SOR) are used. The iteration is written as:

$$u^{(k+1)} = T \, u^{(k)} + C$$

- **Iterative Convergence:** A sequence $\{u^{(k)}\}$ converges if $\|u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)}\| \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$.
- **Condition for Convergence (Spectral Radius Criterion):** The iteration converges **if and only if** the spectral radius $\rho(T) < 1$.
- **Rate of Convergence:** Measured by the **asymptotic error constant** $R = -\log_{10}(\rho(T))$. The smaller $\rho(T)$, the faster the convergence.
- **Termination Criterion:** Iteration stops when one of the following is satisfied:
  - $\|u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)}\| < \epsilon_1$ (absolute tolerance)
  - $\|u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)}\| / \|u^{(k+1)}\| < \epsilon_2$ (relative tolerance)
  - $k > k_{\max}$ (max iterations reached)

---

## 1.9 Boundary and Initial Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Boundary Conditions (BCs):** Specified values of $u$ or its derivatives on the *spatial* boundaries of the domain (e.g., no-slip wall $u=0$, far-field $u=U_\infty$).
> - **Initial Conditions (ICs):** Specified values of $u$ at the *starting time* $t=0$ for time-dependent problems.

In FDM, BCs/ICs are *enforced directly* by replacing the algebraic equation at the boundary node with the prescribed value, or by constructing "ghost" equations that incorporate the derivative condition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why These Concepts Matter in CFD

Every CFD code — from a simple 1D heat conduction solver in MATLAB to ANSYS Fluent solving the compressible Navier-Stokes equations on a billion-cell mesh — is built on the finite difference framework you are studying here. The stability and accuracy of the simulation are *dictated* by the formulas in this section.

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1: Truncate the Taylor Series
Expand $u(x_i + h)$ and $u(x_i - h)$ about $x_i$ and keep only the leading terms.

### Step 2: Algebraically Combine
Add and subtract the two expansions to isolate $u'(x_i)$ or $u''(x_i)$.

### Step 3: Solve for the Derivative
Rearrange to express $u'(x_i)$ (or $u''$) purely in terms of function values at neighboring nodes.

### Step 4: Identify the Truncation Error
The first term you discarded determines the order of accuracy.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU exam paper on this module.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Truncation Error | Order |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Difference, $u'$ | $\dfrac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{h}$ | $-\dfrac{h}{2} u''(x_i)$ | $O(h)$ |
| 2 | Backward Difference, $u'$ | $\dfrac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{h}$ | $+\dfrac{h}{2} u''(x_i)$ | $O(h)$ |
| 3 | Central Difference, $u'$ | $\dfrac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2h}$ | $-\dfrac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| 4 | Central Difference, $u''$ | $\dfrac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^2}$ | $-\dfrac{h^2}{12} u^{(4)}(x_i)$ | $O(h^2)$ |
| 5 | Forward Difference, $u''$ | $\dfrac{u_{i+2} - 2u_{i+1} + u_i}{h^2}$ | $+h u'''(x_i)$ | $O(h)$ |
| 6 | Backward Difference, $u''$ | $\dfrac{u_i - 2u_{i-1} + u_{i-2}}{h^2}$ | $-h u'''(x_i)$ | $O(h)$ |
| 7 | 4th-order Central, $u''$ | $\dfrac{-u_{i+2} + 16u_{i+1} - 30u_i + 16u_{i-1} - u_{i-2}}{12 h^2}$ | $\dfrac{h^4}{90} u^{(6)}$ | $O(h^4)$ |
| 8 | Forward Time, Central Space (FTCS) — Heat Eq. | $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n + r \left( u_{i+1}^n - 2u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n \right)$, $r = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{h^2}$ | $O(\Delta t, h^2)$ | Stable if $r \le \tfrac{1}{2}$ |
| 9 | Von Neumann Stability (Growth Factor) | $G = 1 - 4r \sin^2(kh/2)$ | $\vert G \vert \le 1$ required | — |
| 10 | Iterative Convergence Condition | $\rho(T) < 1$ (spectral radius of iteration matrix) | — | — |
| 11 | Rate of Convergence | $R = -\log_{10} \rho(T)$ | — | — |
| 12 | L2-norm residual termination | $\left( \sum_i (u_i^{k+1} - u_i^{k})^2 \right)^{1/2} < \epsilon$ | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When writing the truncation error, *always* state the leading omitted term explicitly. Students who just write "$O(h^2)$" without showing the coefficient lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 2.4 Application in Engineering

| CFD Application | Role of Finite Differences |
|---|---|
| **Heat Exchanger Design** | Solves 2D heat equation $\nabla^2 T = 0$ on cross-section |
| **Aerodynamics (Panel Methods)** | Solves potential flow $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ on airfoil surface |
| **Combustion Modeling** | Solves species transport PDEs in reactors |
| **Weather Prediction** | Solves primitive equations on global icosahedral grid |
| **Pipe Flow** | Solves 1D momentum equation with central differences |

---

## 2.5 Spectral Radius — Physical Meaning

> [!NOTE]
> The **spectral radius** $\rho(T) = \max \vert \lambda_i \vert$ is the largest absolute eigenvalue of the iteration matrix $T$. Physically, it represents the *slowest-decaying error mode*. If $\rho(T) \ge 1$, the slowest error mode never decays — the iteration will not converge.

---

## 2.6 Round-Off Error Quantification

If a computer stores numbers to $t$ significant digits, the round-off error in a single arithmetic operation is bounded by $0.5 \times 10^{-t}$ times the magnitude of the operand. Over $N$ operations, the cumulative round-off error grows like $\sqrt{N} \times 10^{-t}$ (statistical model) or $N \times 10^{-t}$ (worst case).

For double precision ($t \approx 16$), this is roughly $\sim 10^{-16}$ per operation — small, but disastrous over millions of grid cells in a 3D simulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Difference Formula for $u'(x_i)$ (Taylor's Series Method)

We start from the Taylor expansion of $u(x)$ about $x_i$:

$$u(x_i + h) = u(x_i) + h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2!} u''(x_i) + \frac{h^3}{3!} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{4!} u^{(4)}(x_i) + O(h^5)$$

**Step 1: Solve for $u'(x_i)$.**

Rearranging the above equation:

$$h u'(x_i) = u(x_i + h) - u(x_i) - \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) - \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) - \frac{h^4}{24} u^{(4)}(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 2: Divide both sides by $h$.**

$$u'(x_i) = \frac{u(x_i + h) - u(x_i)}{h} - \frac{h}{2} u''(x_i) - \frac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 3: Drop terms of $O(h)$ and higher.**

$$u'(x_i) \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{h}$$

**Step 4: Identify the truncation error.**

The leading omitted term is $-\dfrac{h}{2} u''(x_i)$, so the truncation error is $O(h)$ — the formula is **first-order accurate**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backward Difference Formula for $u'(x_i)$

The backward Taylor expansion:

$$u(x_i - h) = u(x_i) - h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) - \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + \dots$$

**Step 1: Solve for $u'(x_i)$.**

$$u(x_i) - u(x_i - h) = h u'(x_i) - \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) + \dots$$

**Step 2: Divide by $h$.**

$$u'(x_i) = \frac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{h} + \frac{h}{2} u''(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 3: Truncate.**

$$u'(x_i) \approx \frac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{h}, \quad \text{with error } +\frac{h}{2} u''(x_i)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Central Difference Formula for $u'(x_i)$ (The Workhorse)

Start with both expansions:

$$u(x_i + h) = u(x_i) + h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) + \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{24} u^{(4)}(x_i) + \dots$$

$$u(x_i - h) = u(x_i) - h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) - \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{24} u^{(4)}(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 1: Subtract the second from the first.**

$$u(x_i + h) - u(x_i - h) = 2 h u'(x_i) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^5}{120} u^{(5)}(x_i) + \dots$$

**Step 2: Solve for $u'(x_i)$.**

$$u'(x_i) = \frac{u(x_i + h) - u(x_i - h)}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i) - \frac{h^4}{120} u^{(5)}(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 3: Truncate after the first term.**

$$u'(x_i) \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2h}$$

**Step 4: Truncation error.**

The leading term omitted is $-\dfrac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i)$, so the error is $O(h^2)$ — **second-order accurate**, twice as good as forward/backward differences.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Central Difference for $u''(x_i)$

**Step 1: Add the two Taylor expansions.**

$$u(x_i + h) + u(x_i - h) = 2 u(x_i) + h^2 u''(x_i) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^4}{24} u^{(4)}(x_i) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^6}{720} u^{(6)}(x_i) + \dots$$

**Step 2: Solve for $u''(x_i)$.**

$$u''(x_i) = \frac{u(x_i + h) - 2 u(x_i) + u(x_i - h)}{h^2} - \frac{h^2}{12} u^{(4)}(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 3: Truncate.**

$$u''(x_i) \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - 2 u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^2}, \quad \text{Error: } -\frac{h^2}{12} u^{(4)}(x_i)$$

This is $O(h^2)$ accurate — the famous "second derivative" stencil.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Polynomial Fitting Approach for Forward Difference

Assume a quadratic polynomial passing through three points: $u_{i-1}, u_i, u_{i+1}$.

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$$

> For simplicity, place $x_i$ at origin (so $x_{i-1} = -h, x_i = 0, x_{i+1} = +h$). This doesn't change the derivative.

**Step 1: Enforce the polynomial passes through the three points.**

$$u_{i-1} = a_0 - a_1 h + a_2 h^2$$
$$u_i = a_0$$
$$u_{i+1} = a_0 + a_1 h + a_2 h^2$$

**Step 2: Solve for the coefficients.**

From the second equation: $a_0 = u_i$.

Adding the first and third equations:
$$u_{i-1} + u_{i+1} = 2 a_0 + 2 a_2 h^2 \implies a_2 = \frac{u_{i-1} - 2 u_i + u_{i+1}}{2 h^2}$$

Subtracting the first from the third:
$$u_{i+1} - u_{i-1} = 2 a_1 h \implies a_1 = \frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2 h}$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the polynomial and evaluate at $x_i = 0$.**

$$u'(x) = a_1 + 2 a_2 x \implies u'(x_i) = a_1 = \frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2 h}$$

This **recovers the central difference formula** through a completely different method — confirming its validity.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Von Neumann Stability Analysis for FTCS Scheme

Consider the 1D heat equation:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

Discretize using FTCS:

$$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} = \alpha \frac{u_{i+1}^n - 2 u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n}{h^2}$$

Define the dimensionless number $r = \alpha \Delta t / h^2$. Then:

$$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n + r \left( u_{i+1}^n - 2 u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n \right)$$

**Step 1: Assume a Fourier mode solution.**

$$u_i^n = G^n e^{I k x_i}, \quad \text{where } I = \sqrt{-1}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the difference equation.**

$$G^{n+1} e^{I k x_i} = G^n e^{I k x_i} + r G^n \left( e^{I k (x_i + h)} - 2 e^{I k x_i} + e^{I k (x_i - h)} \right)$$

Dividing by $G^n e^{I k x_i}$:

$$G = 1 + r \left( e^{I k h} - 2 + e^{-I k h} \right) = 1 + r \left( 2 \cos(kh) - 2 \right)$$

**Step 3: Apply the identity $1 - \cos\theta = 2 \sin^2(\theta/2)$.**

$$G = 1 - 4 r \sin^2\left(\frac{k h}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4: Stability requires $|G| \le 1$.**

The maximum of $\sin^2(kh/2)$ is $1$, so the worst case is:
$$G_{\min} = 1 - 4r \ge -1 \implies r \le \frac{1}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CFL/Stability Result:** The FTCS scheme for the heat equation is **conditionally stable** with the constraint $r = \alpha \Delta t / h^2 \le 1/2$. This is the explicit form of the **CFL condition** for diffusion.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Spectral Radius Convergence Theorem

For the linear iteration $u^{(k+1)} = T u^{(k)} + C$:

**Step 1: Decompose the error.** Let $e^{(k)} = u^{(k)} - u^*$, where $u^*$ is the exact fixed point satisfying $u^* = T u^* + C$.

Then:
$$e^{(k+1)} = u^{(k+1)} - u^* = T u^{(k)} + C - T u^* - C = T \left( u^{(k)} - u^* \right) = T e^{(k)}$$

**Step 2: Iterate.**

$$e^{(k)} = T^k e^{(0)}$$

**Step 3: Bound the norm using the spectral radius.**

In any induced norm:
$$\|e^{(k)}\| \le \|T\|^k \|e^{(0)}\|$$

In the spectral norm, $\|T\| = \rho(T)$. Therefore:
$$\|e^{(k)}\| \le [\rho(T)]^k \|e^{(0)}\|$$

**Step 4: Convergence condition.**

$e^{(k)} \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ **if and only if** $\rho(T) < 1$.

If $\rho(T) \ge 1$, there exists at least one eigenvalue of magnitude $\ge 1$, and the corresponding error mode does not decay (or grows), preventing convergence.

**Step 5: Rate of convergence.**

The error decays as $[\rho(T)]^k$. The number of digits of accuracy gained per iteration is:
$$R = -\log_{10} \rho(T) \quad \text{(digits per iteration)}$$

For example, if $\rho(T) = 0.1$, then $R = 1$ digit per iteration — fast convergence. If $\rho(T) = 0.9$, then $R \approx 0.046$ — painfully slow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    E --> F[Apply Boundary and Initial Conditions]
    F --> G{Choose Solver}
    G -->|Direct| H[LU / Gauss Elimination]
    G -->|Iterative| I[Jacobi / Gauss-Seidel / SOR]
    H --> J[Check Convergence and Stability]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Post-Process Numerical Solution]
    K --> L[End]

    style L fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000

```

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TotalError[Total Numerical Error]
        DE[Discretization Error<br>Exact discrete minus exact PDE]
        TE[Truncation Error<br>Local formula error per step]
        RE[Round-Off Error<br>Finite arithmetic precision]
    end
    
    TE -->|reduces as h decreases| Smaller_h[Smaller h]
    RE -->|increases as h decreases| Smaller_h
    DE -->|decreases as h decreases| Smaller_h
    
    Smaller_h --> Optimum[Optimal h: minimum total error]
    
    style DE fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#000
    style TE fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000
    style RE fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46,color:#000
    style Optimum fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram shows the classical "U-shaped" total error curve: discretization/truncation error dominates at large $h$, round-off error dominates at very small $h$, and the optimal grid spacing $h^*$ lies at the minimum of the sum.

## 4.4 Stability, Consistency, Convergence — The CFL Triangle

```mermaid
graph LR
    C[Consistency<br>TE h dt to 0] --> L[Lax Equivalence Theorem]

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lax's Theorem (recap):** For a *well-posed linear* initial value problem with a *consistent* scheme, **stability $\iff$ convergence**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Explain the Taylor's series approach for deriving finite difference approximations. What is truncation error?

**Model Answer:**

The Taylor's series approach expands a function $u(x)$ about a chosen point $x_i$ in a power series of the step size $h$:

$$u(x_i + h) = u(x_i) + h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2!} u''(x_i) + \frac{h^3}{3!} u'''(x_i) + \dots$$

By truncating the series at a chosen order and solving algebraically for the desired derivative $u'(x_i)$ (or $u''(x_i)$, etc.), we obtain a finite difference approximation expressed in terms of function values at neighboring grid points.

**Truncation error** is the difference between the exact derivative and the finite difference approximation. It equals the first term that was discarded in the Taylor series. For example, for the forward difference $u'(x_i) \approx (u_{i+1} - u_i)/h$, the truncation error is $-\tfrac{h}{2} u''(x_i)$, making it $O(h)$ accurate. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State and explain Lax's Equivalence Theorem.

**Model Answer:**

**Lax's Equivalence Theorem:** *For a well-posed linear initial value problem and a consistent finite difference scheme, stability is the necessary and sufficient condition for convergence.*

This means:
- A scheme is **consistent** if its truncation error tends to zero as the grid spacing $h$ and time step $\Delta t$ tend to zero.
- A scheme is **stable** if errors (round-off or initial perturbations) do not grow without bound as the computation proceeds.
- **Convergence** means the numerical solution approaches the exact solution of the PDE as $h, \Delta t \to 0$.

The theorem provides the practical bridge: in CFD codes, we *prove* consistency (easy) and *prove* stability (via von Neumann analysis or matrix methods), and convergence is automatically guaranteed. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the central difference approximation for the first derivative $u'(x_i)$ using the Taylor's series approach. State clearly the truncation error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the one-dimensional heat equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$, derive the stability condition of the explicit FTCS scheme using the von Neumann method. What is the maximum allowable time step if $\alpha = 0.1 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and $h = 0.05 \text{ m}$? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1: Write the forward and backward Taylor expansions about $x_i$.**

$$u(x_i + h) = u(x_i) + h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) + \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{24} u^{(4)}(x_i) + O(h^5)$$

$$u(x_i - h) = u(x_i) - h u'(x_i) + \frac{h^2}{2} u''(x_i) - \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + \frac{h^4}{24} u^{(4)}(x_i) - O(h^5)$$

**Step 2: Subtract the second from the first.**

$$u(x_i + h) - u(x_i - h) = 2 h u'(x_i) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^3}{6} u'''(x_i) + 2 \cdot \frac{h^5}{120} u^{(5)}(x_i) + \dots$$

**Step 3: Solve for $u'(x_i)$.**

$$u'(x_i) = \frac{u(x_i + h) - u(x_i - h)}{2h} - \frac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i) - \frac{h^4}{120} u^{(5)}(x_i) - \dots$$

**Step 4: Truncate after the first term.**

$$u'(x_i) \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2h}$$

**Step 5: Truncation error.**

The leading omitted term is $-\dfrac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i)$, so the truncation error is $O(h^2)$, and the formula is **second-order accurate**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1: Discretize the heat equation using FTCS.**

$$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} = \alpha \frac{u_{i+1}^n - 2 u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n}{h^2}$$

Rearrange with $r = \alpha \Delta t / h^2$:

$$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n + r \left( u_{i+1}^n - 2 u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n \right)$$

**Step 2: Assume a Fourier mode solution** $u_i^n = G^n e^{I k x_i}$.

**Step 3: Substitute and divide by $G^n e^{I k x_i}$.**

$$G = 1 + r \left( e^{I k h} - 2 + e^{-I k h} \right) = 1 + 2r (\cos(kh) - 1)$$

**Step 4: Apply the identity $1 - \cos\theta = 2 \sin^2(\theta/2)$.**

$$G = 1 - 4 r \sin^2\left(\frac{k h}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Apply the stability criterion $|G| \le 1$.**

The minimum value of $G$ occurs when $\sin^2(kh/2) = 1$:

$$G_{\min} = 1 - 4r$$

Stability requires $G_{\min} \ge -1$, i.e., $1 - 4r \ge -1$, giving:

$$r \le \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6: Compute the maximum allowable $\Delta t$.**

$$r = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{h^2} \le \frac{1}{2} \implies \Delta t \le \frac{h^2}{2 \alpha} = \frac{(0.05)^2}{2 \times 0.1} = \frac{0.0025}{0.2} = 0.0125 \text{ s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The FTCS scheme is conditionally stable for $r \le 1/2$. The maximum allowable time step is **$\Delta t_{\max} = 0.0125$ s**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is the polynomial fitting approach? Using this method, derive the central difference formula for $u''(x_i)$ using a quadratic polynomial $u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$ that passes through $(x_{i-1}, u_{i-1})$, $(x_i, u_i)$, $(x_{i+1}, u_{i+1})$. Assume the grid is uniform with spacing $h$ and $x_i = 0$ for convenience. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the iterative convergence condition using the spectral radius. Define the rate of convergence and state the termination criteria for an iterative solver. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Step 1: Set up the polynomial.**

$$u(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$$

**Step 2: Place the origin at $x_i$, so $x_{i-1} = -h, x_i = 0, x_{i+1} = +h$.** The three conditions are:

$$u(-h) = a_0 - a_1 h + a_2 h^2 = u_{i-1}$$
$$u(0) = a_0 = u_i$$
$$u(h) = a_0 + a_1 h + a_2 h^2 = u_{i+1}$$

**Step 3: From the second equation, $a_0 = u_i$.**

**Step 4: Add the first and third equations.**

$$u_{i-1} + u_{i+1} = 2 a_0 + 2 a_2 h^2 = 2 u_i + 2 a_2 h^2$$

Solve for $a_2$:

$$a_2 = \frac{u_{i-1} - 2 u_i + u_{i+1}}{2 h^2}$$

**Step 5: Differentiate twice and evaluate at $x = 0$.**

$$u'(x) = a_1 + 2 a_2 x$$
$$u''(x) = 2 a_2 \quad \text{(independent of } x\text{)}$$

Therefore:
$$u''(x_i) = 2 a_2 = \frac{u_{i-1} - 2 u_i + u_{i+1}}{h^2}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

$$u''(x_i) \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - 2 u_i + u_{i-1}}{h^2}$$

This is the standard central difference formula for the second derivative, $O(h^2)$ accurate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1: Iterative scheme.**

For a linear system $A u = b$, an iterative method is written as:

$$u^{(k+1)} = T u^{(k)} + C$$

where $T$ is the **iteration matrix** and $C$ is a constant vector determined by $A$ and $b$.

**Step 2: Convergence condition.**

Let $u^*$ be the exact solution (fixed point) satisfying $u^* = T u^* + C$. The error $e^{(k)} = u^{(k)} - u^*$ evolves as:

$$e^{(k+1)} = T e^{(k)} \implies e^{(k)} = T^k e^{(0)}$$

**Step 3: Bound the error.**

Using the spectral norm:
$$\|e^{(k)}\| \le \|T\|^k \|e^{(0)}\| = [\rho(T)]^k \|e^{(0)}\|$$

For $e^{(k)} \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$, we need $\rho(T) < 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Condition:** The iteration converges **if and only if the spectral radius** $\rho(T) < 1$. This is both necessary and sufficient.

**Step 4: Rate of convergence.**

The error decays as $[\rho(T)]^k$. The number of correct decimal digits gained per iteration is:

$$R = -\log_{10} \rho(T)$$

Larger $R$ means faster convergence. For example, $\rho(T) = 0.01$ gives $R = 2$ digits/iteration (very fast); $\rho(T) = 0.99$ gives $R \approx 0.004$ digits/iteration (extremely slow).

**Step 5: Termination criteria.**

The iteration is stopped when any of the following is satisfied:

1. **Absolute tolerance:** $\|u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)}\| < \epsilon_1$
2. **Relative tolerance:** $\|u^{(k+1)} - u^{(k)}\| / \|u^{(k+1)}\| < \epsilon_2$
3. **Residual tolerance:** $\|b - A u^{(k+1)}\| < \epsilon_3$
4. **Maximum iterations:** $k \ge k_{\max}$ (safeguard against non-convergence)

> [!TIP]
> In CFD, $\epsilon_2 \sim 10^{-6}$ (relative) is a common default, with $k_{\max}$ set to 10,000 or higher for stiff problems. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make on this module — and exactly where marks are lost:**
>
> 1. **Confusing truncation error with discretization error.** Truncation error is *local* (per step, from the Taylor series). Discretization error is *global* (the difference between the exact continuous and exact discrete solutions). They differ by a factor related to the number of steps. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the coefficient in $O(h^n)$ statements.** A student writes "$u'(x_i) = (u_{i+1} - u_{i-1})/(2h)$ with error $O(h^2)$." The examiner wants the *leading term* of the error, e.g., $-\tfrac{h^2}{6} u'''(x_i)$, not just the order. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 3. **Confusing $r = \alpha \Delta t / h^2$ with the Courant number $C = u \Delta t / h$.** The $r$ is for the **heat/diffusion equation**; $C$ is for the **advection/wave equation**. Mixing them is a fatal error. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Writing $\rho(T) \le 1$ instead of $\rho(T) < 1$ for convergence.** The strict inequality is required (at $\rho = 1$, the error does not decay). **(−1 mark)**
>
> 5. **Omitting the assumption of linearity for Lax's Theorem.** Lax's equivalence requires a *linear* PDE. For nonlinear problems, the theorem does not apply directly. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and keep it beside you during revision.**

- [x] **Taylor's Series:** Expand $u(x_i \pm h)$, solve algebraically, truncate.
- [x] **Polynomial Fitting:** Assume $u(x) = \sum a_k x^k$, fit through neighbors, differentiate.
- [x] **Forward Difference:** $u'_i \approx (u_{i+1} - u_i)/h$, error $O(h)$.
- [x] **Backward Difference:** $u'_i \approx (u_i - u_{i-1})/h$, error $O(h)$.
- [x] **Central Difference (1st derivative):** $u'_i \approx (u_{i+1} - u_{i-1})/(2h)$, error $O(h^2)$.
- [x] **Central Difference (2nd derivative):** $u''_i \approx (u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1})/h^2$, error $O(h^2)$.
- [x] **Higher-order central second derivative:** 5-point stencil with $O(h^4)$ accuracy.
- [x] **Truncation Error:** First dropped term in Taylor series; $O(h^n)$.
- [x] **Discretization Error:** Exact-discrete minus exact-continuous; includes accumulated TE.
- [x] **Round-Off Error:** From finite word length; grows as $h$ shrinks.
- [x] **Optimal $h$:** Where discretization + round-off error is minimized (U-curve).
- [x] **Consistency:** TE $\to 0$ as $h, \Delta t \to 0$. Required for convergence.
- [x] **Stability:** Errors don't grow. Tested by **von Neumann** ($|G| \le 1$) or matrix method ($\rho(T) < 1$).
- [x] **Lax Equivalence:** Consistency + Stability $\iff$ Convergence (linear PDE).
- [x] **FTCS Heat Equation:** $G = 1 - 4r \sin^2(kh/2)$; stable if $r = \alpha \Delta t / h^2 \le 1/2$.
- [x] **Iterative Convergence:** $\rho(T) < 1$ (necessary and sufficient for linear systems).
- [x] **Rate of Convergence:** $R = -\log_{10} \rho(T)$ digits/iteration.
- [x] **Termination:** $\|$ change $\|$ < $\epsilon$ (absolute), or $\|$ change $\|$ / $\|$ new $\|$ < $\epsilon$ (relative), or $k = k_{\max}$.
- [x] **Boundary Conditions:** Dirichlet (value), Neumann (derivative), Robin (combination).
- [x] **Initial Conditions:** Values of $u$ at $t = 0$; required for time-marching schemes.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU):** When asked to derive a finite difference formula, always show *both* Taylor expansions (forward and backward) explicitly, label each term, and conclude with **both** the formula and the leading truncation error term. Examiners reward completeness — partial work gets partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Finite Volume Method, Finite Volume Method for Steady One-Dimensional Conduction Problems, One-Dimensional Transient Heat Conduction Problems - Explicit, Implicit, Crank-Nicholson Schemes, Under and Over Relaxations, Handling of Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Finite Volume Method (FVM)

The **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** is a numerical technique used to solve partial differential equations (PDEs) by discretizing the physical domain into a finite number of control volumes and applying the conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy) to each cell. It is the de-facto industry and academic standard for computational fluid dynamics because it guarantees conservation of physical quantities at the discrete level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Strict):**
> The Finite Volume Method is a discretization technique that transforms governing differential equations into algebraic equations by integrating them over discrete control volumes. The resulting discrete equation expresses the **conservation principle** (Conservation of Mass, Momentum, and Energy) for each finite control volume in the domain.

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Water Tank Network"

Imagine a long pipeline divided into several equal cylindrical sections (control volumes). Water flows in from one end and out the other. We don't know the exact velocity profile inside, but we know three things for each cylinder:
1. **Inflow rate** through the left face
2. **Outflow rate** through the right face
3. **Source/Sink** (a tap filling or a drain emptying the cylinder)

By enforcing **"Inflow + Source = Outflow + Storage"** for *every* cylinder individually, we can solve for the unknown flow rate at the boundaries of each section. 

This is exactly what the Finite Volume Method does with heat, mass, or momentum on a computational grid — it forces **conservation** across every small box. The method is *locally* and *globally* conservative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinguishing Feature of FVM:**
> Unlike the Finite Difference Method (FDM), which approximates derivatives directly at grid points, FVM works with **volume integrals** of the conserved quantity. This makes FVM naturally suited for complex, irregular geometries (the basis of commercial codes like ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, and STAR-CCM+).

## 1.2 FVM for Steady One-Dimensional Conduction Problems

The steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction equation (without generation) in a slab is given by:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) = 0$$

For constant thermal conductivity ($k$ = constant), this simplifies to the **Laplace equation**:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$$

The general 1D steady conduction equation with source term is:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + S = 0$$

where $S$ is the volumetric source term (W/m³), and $k$ is the thermal conductivity (W/m·K).

## 1.3 FVM for Transient (Unsteady) One-Dimensional Conduction

The transient, one-dimensional heat conduction equation (no source) is:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)$$

For constant $k$, $\rho$, and $c_p$, this is the **Heat (Diffusion) Equation**:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

where $\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Clarification:** The word "Scheme" in transient problems refers to how we treat the time derivative — whether we evaluate the spatial derivatives (fluxes) at the *old* time level, the *new* time level, or a weighted average of both. This choice defines the **Explicit**, **Implicit**, and **Crank-Nicolson** schemes.

## 1.4 Under and Over Relaxation

Relaxation is a numerical technique used to **accelerate or stabilize** the iterative solution of algebraic equations arising from FVM discretization.

- **Under-Relaxation** ($\omega < 1$): Used to **dampen oscillations** and ensure convergence in nonlinear or stiff problems.
- **Over-Relaxation** ($\omega > 1$): Used to **accelerate convergence** when the system is stable and approaching the solution monotonically (commonly used with Successive Over-Relaxation — SOR).

The relaxed value $\phi_{new}^{relaxed}$ is computed as:

$$\phi_{new}^{relaxed} = \phi_{old} + \omega \left(\phi_{new} - \phi_{old}\right)$$

where $\phi_{new}$ is the value computed from the current iteration, and $\omega$ is the **relaxation factor**.

## 1.5 Handling of Boundary Conditions

Boundary conditions (BCs) in FVM are not merely appended to the system of equations — they are **integrated into the discretization** through fictitious/half control volumes at the domain boundaries. The three classical types of BCs are:

| BC Type | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet** | Prescribed value of $\phi$ | $\phi = \phi_{prescribed}$ |
| **Neumann** | Prescribed flux (derivative) | $-k \frac{d\phi}{dx} = q_{prescribed}$ |
| **Robin / Mixed / Cauchy** | Convection to ambient | $-k \frac{d\phi}{dx} = h(\phi - \phi_\infty)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In FVM, the boundary is always placed at the **midpoint** of the fictitious control volume, not at the grid node. This is a recurring KTU exam pitfall and is covered in detail in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step FVM Discretization Procedure (Conceptual Framework)

The FVM discretization for any conservation equation follows **three rigorous steps**. KTU examiners often award marks simply for naming and explaining these steps.

1. **Step 1: Domain Discretization & Control Volume Formation**
   - Divide the physical domain into a finite number of **non-overlapping control volumes (CVs)**.
   - Each grid node $P$ is surrounded by a CV with neighboring nodes $W$ (West) and $E$ (East) in 1D.
   - The CV faces are denoted $w$ (west face) and $e$ (east face), located midway between nodes.

2. **Step 2: Integration of the Governing Equation Over the CV**
   - Integrate the PDE over the CV and over the time interval $[t, t+\Delta t]$.
   - Apply the **Divergence Theorem** to convert volume integrals of divergence into surface integrals of flux.
   - This yields: *Net flux out + Source = Rate of change of storage.*

3. **Step 3: Profile Assumption & Algebraic Equation**
   - Assume a profile (linear, piece-wise, polynomial) for the variation of $\phi$ between nodes to evaluate the surface integrals.
   - Obtain an algebraic equation relating $\phi$ at node $P$ to its neighbors $W$ and $E$.

## 2.2 Steady 1D Conduction — Discretization Derivation

Consider the 1D steady heat equation with source:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + S = 0$$

Integrate over the CV of node $P$ (from $w$ to $e$):

$$\int_{w}^{e} \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{w}^{e} S \, dx = 0$$

Applying the fundamental theorem of calculus:

$$\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{e} - \left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{w} + \bar{S} \Delta x = 0$$

Assuming a **linear (piece-wise) profile** between nodes, the gradient at each face becomes a finite-difference-like expression:

$$\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{e} = \frac{T_E - T_P}{\delta x_E}, \quad \left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{w} = \frac{T_P - T_W}{\delta x_W}$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + b_P$$

where the coefficients are:

$$a_W = \frac{k_w}{\delta x_W}, \quad a_E = \frac{k_e}{\delta x_E}, \quad a_P = a_W + a_E - S_P \Delta x, \quad b_P = S_u \Delta x$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Generalization Rule:**
> The 1D steady-state FVM discrete equation *always* reduces to the canonical form:
> $$a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + b_P$$
> All boundary conditions, sources, and material property variations are encoded in the **coefficients** $a_W$, $a_E$, $a_P$, and $b_P$ — never in a change of the fundamental structure.

## 2.3 Boundary Condition Implementation — The Four Cases

### Case 1: Dirichlet (Prescribed Temperature) at West Boundary
When $T_W$ is known (e.g., $T_1 = T_A$ at the west face), the standard coefficient equation is *rewritten* to lump the known contribution into the source term $b_P$. The coefficient $a_W$ is set to **zero**, and the contribution $a_W T_W$ is added to $b_P$.

### Case 2: Neumann (Prescribed Heat Flux) at West Boundary
A fictitious half-CV is added. The flux $q_w$ is added directly to $b_P$, and the west face distance becomes $\delta x_W / 2$.

$$b_P \rightarrow b_P + q_w A$$

### Case 3: Convection (Robin) Boundary at West
The convective flux $h(T_\infty - T_P)$ is split:
- A "convective" conductance $h$ is added to $a_W$ (effectively augmenting $a_P$).
- The ambient contribution $h T_\infty$ is added to $b_P$.

### Case 4: Insulated / Symmetry Boundary
The flux is zero, so no contribution is added. The west face of the boundary CV acts as a mirror, and $\delta x_W = 2 \Delta x$ (a half-cell effectively).

## 2.4 Transient Discretization — The Three Schemes

For transient problems, the time derivative is discretized using a finite-difference approximation and the spatial term is treated at different time levels. Let $f$ denote the fraction of time advancement where $f = 0$ (old), $f = 1$ (new), and $f = 0.5$ (Crank-Nicolson).

The general FVM transient equation is:

$$a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + a_P^0 T_P^0 + b_P$$

where $T_P^0$ is the value at the previous time level and $a_P^0 = \frac{\rho c_p \Delta x}{\Delta t}$.

The unified form for all three schemes is obtained by defining modified coefficients:

| Scheme | Time weight $f$ | $a_P$ | $a_W$ | $a_E$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Explicit** | $0$ | $a_P^0$ | $a_W$ (at old time) | $a_E$ (at old time) |
| **Fully Implicit** | $1$ | $a_P^0 + a_W + a_E$ | $a_W$ | $a_E$ |
| **Crank-Nicolson** | $0.5$ | $a_P^0 + f(a_W + a_E)$ | $f a_W$ | $f a_E$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in every KTU CFD paper.**

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1D Steady Conduction PDE | $\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(k\frac{dT}{dx}\right) + S = 0$ | W/m³ for $S$ |
| 2 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ | m²/s |
| 3 | 1D Transient Conduction PDE | $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ | Energy equation |
| 4 | Canonical Discrete Form | $a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + b_P$ | Always true in 1D FVM |
| 5 | Diffusion Coefficients | $a_W = k_w / \delta x_W, \; a_E = k_e / \delta x_E$ | W/K (conductance) |
| 6 | Source Term Split | $S = S_p T_P + S_u$ (must have $S_p \le 0$) | Required for stability |
| 7 | Boundary Source (Neumann) | $b_P = b_P + q_w A$ (west), $b_P = b_P + q_e A$ (east) | $q$ in W/m² |
| 8 | Boundary (Robin) | $a_W \leftarrow a_W + h, \; b_P \leftarrow b_P + h T_\infty$ | $h$ in W/m²K |
| 9 | Transient General Form | $a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + a_P^0 T_P^0 + b_P$ | $a_P^0 = \rho c_p \Delta x / \Delta t$ |
| 10 | Stability (Explicit) | $\Delta t \le \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2\alpha}$ for 1D internal node | **CRITICAL** for KTU |
| 11 | Crank-Nicolson (1D internal) | $a_P^0 (T_P - T_P^0) = f \cdot a_E(T_E - T_P) + (1-f) \cdot a_E(T_E^0 - T_P^0)$ | $f = 0.5$ |
| 12 | Relaxation Formula | $\phi^{relaxed} = \phi^{old} + \omega(\phi^{new} - \phi^{old})$ | $\omega \in [0, 2]$ |
| 13 | Under-Relaxation Range | $0 < \omega < 1$ (typically 0.5 to 0.9) | Damping |
| 14 | Over-Relaxation Range | $1 < \omega < 2$ (typically 1.1 to 1.7) | Acceleration (SOR) |
| 15 | Optimized SOR factor | $\omega_{opt} = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho(B)^2}}$ | Depends on spectral radius $\rho(B)$ |
| 16 | Heat Balance (Residual) | $R_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + b_P - a_P T_P$ | Should $\to 0$ at convergence |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The FVM and these schemes are the *backbone* of virtually every thermal and CFD simulation used in industry:

- **Electronics Cooling:** Predicting junction temperatures in CPUs, GPUs, and power modules using transient implicit schemes (for unconditional stability over long time scales).
- **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle thermal protection system (TPS) ablation analysis using Crank-Nicolson for second-order accuracy in time.
- **Nuclear Reactor Safety:** Transient temperature fields in fuel rods under loss-of-coolant accidents (LOCAs) — strict use of implicit schemes for stability.
- **HVAC & Building Sciences:** Steady-state wall/roof heat transfer for energy-efficient building design.
- **Metal Casting & Welding:** Solidification problems requiring explicit schemes with very small time steps for capturing sharp phase-change interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: FVM Discretization of the 1D Steady Conduction Equation

We begin with the governing PDE:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + S = 0$$

**Step 1 — Discretize the domain.** Let the grid nodes be labeled $W$, $P$, $E$ (west, central, east) with control volume $P$ bounded by faces $w$ and $e$. Let $\Delta x = x_e - x_w$ be the width of the CV around $P$. Let $\delta x_W = x_P - x_W$ and $\delta x_E = x_E - x_P$.

**Step 2 — Integrate the PDE over the CV $[x_w, x_e]$:**

$$\int_{x_w}^{x_e} \frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{x_w}^{x_e} S \, dx = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the fundamental theorem of calculus to the diffusion term:**

$$\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{x_e} - \left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{x_w} + \bar{S} \Delta x = 0$$

Here, $\bar{S}$ is the average value of the source term over the CV. For uniform $S$:

$$\bar{S} \Delta x = S \Delta x$$

**Step 4 — Approximate the face gradients using a piece-wise linear profile:**

$$\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{e} \approx \frac{T_E - T_P}{\delta x_E}, \quad \left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_{w} \approx \frac{T_P - T_W}{\delta x_W}$$

Assuming constant $k$ over each face (so $k_e$ and $k_w$ are face values):

$$\frac{k_e (T_E - T_P)}{\delta x_E} - \frac{k_w (T_P - T_W)}{\delta x_W} + S \Delta x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to the canonical FVM form:**

$$\left(\frac{k_w}{\delta x_W} + \frac{k_e}{\delta x_E}\right) T_P = \frac{k_w}{\delta x_W} T_W + \frac{k_e}{\delta x_E} T_E + S \Delta x$$

Substituting the standard coefficient notation:

$$a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + b_P$$

where

$$a_W = \frac{k_w}{\delta x_W}, \quad a_E = \frac{k_e}{\delta x_E}, \quad a_P = a_W + a_E, \quad b_P = S \Delta x$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for non-uniform source $S = S_p T_P + S_u$:** The source term is split so that the $T_P$-dependent part migrates to the LHS. Then:
> $$a_P = a_W + a_E - S_p \Delta x, \quad b_P = S_u \Delta x$$
> The KTU-required stability condition $S_p \le 0$ ensures the diagonal dominance is preserved.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transient Discretization — The Three Schemes

Starting from:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + S$$

Integrate over the CV $[x_w, x_e]$ and time $[t, t+\Delta t]$:

$$\int_t^{t+\Delta t} \int_{x_w}^{x_e} \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} dx \, dt = \int_t^{t+\Delta t} \left[\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_e - \left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_w + \bar{S} \Delta x\right] dt$$

The LHS gives the storage term:

$$\rho c_p \Delta x (T_P - T_P^0)$$

Rearranging:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} (T_P - T_P^0) = \left[\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_e - \left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_w\right]_{avg} + \bar{S} \Delta x$$

Define the *old-time* diffusion flux as $F_{old}$ and the *new-time* diffusion flux as $F_{new}$. The weighted average is:

$$F = f \cdot F_{new} + (1-f) \cdot F_{old}$$

**Case A: Explicit Scheme ($f = 0$).** Spatial fluxes are evaluated at the old time level:

$$a_P^0 (T_P - T_P^0) = a_W T_W^0 + a_E T_E^0 + b_P - a_P^0 T_P^0$$

Solving for $T_P$:

$$T_P = T_P^0 + \frac{\Delta t}{\rho c_p \Delta x}\left[a_W T_W^0 + a_E T_E^0 + b_P - a_P^0 T_P^0\right]$$

With $a_P^0 = a_W + a_E$ (diagonal coefficient *only*):

$$T_P = \frac{a_W}{a_P^0} T_W^0 + \frac{a_E}{a_P^0} T_E^0 + \frac{b_P}{a_P^0}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Stability Criterion (KTU Favourite):** The explicit scheme is **conditionally stable**. For 1D internal node:
> $$\Delta t \le \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2\alpha}$$
> If this is violated, the solution will diverge and produce physically meaningless oscillations (negative temperatures, etc.).

**Case B: Fully Implicit Scheme ($f = 1$).** Spatial fluxes are evaluated at the new time level:

$$a_P^0 T_P - a_P^0 T_P^0 = a_W (T_P - T_W) + a_E (T_P - T_E) + b_P$$

Wait — careful! The implicit scheme in FVM canonical form is:

$$a_P^0 (T_P - T_P^0) = a_W (T_W - T_P) + a_E (T_E - T_P) + b_P$$

Rearranging:

$$(a_P^0 + a_W + a_E) T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + a_P^0 T_P^0 + b_P$$

So the diagonal coefficient becomes:

$$a_P = a_P^0 + a_W + a_E$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The implicit scheme is unconditionally stable** for any $\Delta t$, but accuracy decreases for very large $\Delta t$ (only first-order accurate in time).

**Case C: Crank-Nicolson Scheme ($f = 0.5$).** Spatial fluxes are the average of old and new:

$$a_P^0 (T_P - T_P^0) = f [a_W (T_W - T_P) + a_E (T_E - T_P)] + (1-f) [a_W (T_W^0 - T_P^0) + a_E (T_E^0 - T_P^0)] + b_P$$

Substituting $f = 0.5$ and collecting terms:

$$(a_P^0 + 0.5 a_W + 0.5 a_E) T_P = 0.5 a_W T_W + 0.5 a_E T_E + a_P^0 T_P^0 + 0.5 a_W T_W^0 + 0.5 a_E T_E^0 - 0.5 (a_W + a_E) T_P^0 + b_P$$

The diagonal coefficient is:

$$a_P = a_P^0 + f \cdot a_W + f \cdot a_E = a_P^0 + 0.5(a_W + a_E)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crank-Nicolson is second-order accurate in both space and time**, making it the most accurate of the three. However, it can produce **spurious oscillations** for very steep gradients or large time steps — students must mention this trade-off in KTU answers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Boundary Condition — Convection at West Face

At the west boundary, the control volume has a half-width $\delta x_W / 2$. The convective BC is:

$$-k \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = h (T_\infty - T_P)$$

The diffusion flux into the west face of the CV (outward normal points in $-x$, hence the negative sign convention):

$$q_w = -k \frac{T_P - T_W}{\delta x_W / 2} = \frac{2k}{\delta x_W}(T_W - T_P)$$

Equating this with the convective flux gives:

$$\frac{2k}{\delta x_W}(T_P - T_W) = h(T_\infty - T_P)$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{2k}{\delta x_W} + h\right) T_P = \frac{2k}{\delta x_W} T_W + h T_\infty$$

Comparing to the canonical form $a_P T_P = a_W T_W + b_P$:

$$a_W = \frac{2k}{\delta x_W}, \quad a_P = a_W + h, \quad b_P = h T_\infty$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The factor of 2 in $\frac{2k}{\delta x_W}$ arises because the fictitious boundary CV has half the normal width. This is a classic KTU question. Forgetting this factor leads to incorrect temperatures and is a guaranteed mark-deduction zone.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Under and Over Relaxation

Consider a system solved iteratively. The new value of $\phi$ from iteration $n+1$ is $\phi^{n+1}_{computed}$, while the previous value is $\phi^n$. The relaxed value is:

$$\phi^{n+1}_{relaxed} = \phi^n + \omega (\phi^{n+1}_{computed} - \phi^n)$$

Expanding:

$$\phi^{n+1}_{relaxed} = (1 - \omega) \phi^n + \omega \, \phi^{n+1}_{computed}$$

**Analysis of cases:**
- $\omega = 1$: No relaxation; $\phi^{n+1}_{relaxed} = \phi^{n+1}_{computed}$. Standard Gauss-Seidel/Jacobi.
- $0 < \omega < 1$: **Under-relaxation.** The new value is a weighted average heavily favoring the old value. This **damps oscillations** and ensures convergence for nonlinear/stiff problems.
- $1 < \omega < 2$: **Over-relaxation.** The new value is extrapolated beyond the computed step, accelerating convergence. Used in Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR) for elliptic problems.
- $\omega \ge 2$: Divergence.

For SOR, the optimal relaxation factor for a 1D problem of size $N$ is approximately:

$$\omega_{opt} = \frac{2}{1 + \sin(\pi / N)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "under" and "over" relaxation. Memorize: **Under** = small $\omega$ = slow but stable; **Over** = large $\omega$ = fast but risky. In commercial CFD codes (Fluent, OpenFOAM), under-relaxation is the default for pressure, momentum, and turbulence quantities.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Transient Schemes (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Explicit | Fully Implicit | Crank-Nicolson |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time weight $f$** | 0 | 1 | 0.5 |
| **Spatial derivative evaluated at** | Old time | New time | Average of old and new |
| **Diagonal coefficient $a_P$** | $a_P^0$ | $a_P^0 + a_W + a_E$ | $a_P^0 + 0.5(a_W + a_E)$ |
| **Stability** | **Conditionally stable** | **Unconditionally stable** | **Conditionally stable** (oscillations for large $\Delta t$) |
| **Stability limit (1D)** | $\Delta t \le \Delta x^2 / (2\alpha)$ | None | $\Delta t \le \Delta x^2 / \alpha$ (looser than explicit) |
| **Time accuracy** | First order | First order | **Second order** |
| **Computational cost per step** | Trivial (no system solve) | Requires matrix inversion | Requires matrix inversion |
| **Best use case** | Quick transient, small $\Delta t$ | Long transients, stiff problems | High-accuracy wave/propagation |
| **KTU score weight** | ⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FVM Grid Topology (1D Control Volume Layout)

The following block-level schematic maps the topology of a typical 1D FVM grid, showing the relative positions of nodes, CV faces, and the canonical stencil used in discretization.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef node fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000;
    classDef face fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000;
    classDef domain fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000;
    classDef bc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000;

    WB[West Boundary Node W1]:::bc
    W2[West Node W]:::node
    P1[Central Node P]:::node
    E1[East Node E]:::node
    EB[East Boundary Node N]:::bc

    fwWest[Face w]:::face
    fwEast[Face e]:::face
    fwLeft[Face at WB]:::face
    fwRight[Face at EB]:::face

    CVP[Control Volume around P]:::domain
    CVW[Control Volume around W]:::domain

    WB --- fwLeft
    fwLeft --- W2
    W2 --- fwWest
    fwWest --- P1
    P1 --- fwEast
    fwEast --- E1
    E1 --- fwRight
    fwRight --- EB

    W2 -. encloses .-> CVW
    P1 -. encloses .-> CVP

    note1[Boundary is at the midpoint of the half CV, not at the node]:::bc
    WB -.-> note1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight:** Note the position of the boundary — it lies at the *midpoint* of the half/fictitious control volume, not at the boundary node itself. This is the cornerstone of FVM boundary treatment and a frequent KTU question.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: FVM Discretization Pipeline

This diagram maps the sequential computational steps from the physical PDE to the solved temperature field, illustrating how the FVM processes information in a CFD solver.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000;
    classDef process fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000;
    classDef storage fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000;
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000;
    classDef decision fill:#e2d5f7,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000;

    PDE[Governing PDE: 1D Heat Equation]:::input
    BC[Boundary Conditions: Dirichlet / Neumann / Robin]:::input
    MESH[Generate 1D Grid and Control Volumes]:::process
    INT[Integrate PDE over Control Volume]:::process
    DIV[Apply Divergence Theorem]:::process
    PROF[Assume Profile: Piecewise Linear]:::process
    ALG[Derive Algebraic Equation aP TP = aW TW + aE TE + bP]:::storage
    COEFF[Compute Coefficients aW, aE, aP, bP]:::process
    BCINTEGRATE[Integrate BC into Coefficients and Source]:::process
    ASSEM[Assemble Global Tridiagonal System AT = b]:::process
    DECIDE{Steady or Transient?}:::decision
    STEADYSOLVE[Direct Solve: TDMA Thomas Algorithm]:::process
    TRANSSCHEME[Choose Time Scheme: Explicit Implicit CrankNicolson]:::process
    STEPUPDATE[Time Marching Step Update]:::process
    RELAX{Apply Relaxation?}:::decision
    RELAXCALC[Compute omega for SOR or Under Relaxation]:::process
    CONVERGE{Convergence Check: Residual less than tolerance}:::decision
    OUTPUT[Temperature Field T x and T t]:::output
    POST[Post Processing: Plots, Isotherms, Fluxes]:::output

    PDE --> MESH
    BC --> MESH
    MESH --> INT
    INT --> DIV
    DIV --> PROF
    PROF --> ALG
    ALG --> COEFF
    BC --> BCINTEGRATE
    BCINTEGRATE --> COEFF
    COEFF --> ASSEM
    ASSEM --> DECIDE

    DECIDE -->|Steady State| STEADYSOLVE
    DECIDE -->|Transient| TRANSSCHEME
    TRANSSCHEME --> STEPUPDATE
    STEPUPDATE --> RELAX
    RELAX -->|Yes| RELAXCALC
    RELAX -->|No| CONVERGE
    RELAXCALC --> CONVERGE
    STEADYSOLVE --> CONVERGE
    CONVERGE -->|Not Converged| STEADYSOLVE
    CONVERGE -->|Converged| OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> POST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** The pipeline clearly shows the **branching logic** between steady (direct solve) and transient (time-marching) simulations. The relaxation block is a *loop-around* that can be applied to either branch when iterative solution is required.

## 4.3 Comparative Logic Map: The Three Transient Schemes

This diagram compares the three transient schemes in a side-by-side flow chart to highlight the difference in how each scheme handles the time derivative.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef explicit fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000;
    classDef implicit fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000;
    classDef crank fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000;
    classDef common fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000;

    INIT[Initial Condition: T at t = 0]:::common

    EX[Explicit Scheme: f = 0]:::explicit
    IM[Implicit Scheme: f = 1]:::implicit
    CN[Crank Nicolson Scheme: f = 0.5]:::crank

    EXA[Evaluate Fluxes at Old Time Level]:::explicit
    IMA[Evaluate Fluxes at New Time Level]:::implicit
    CNA[Evaluate Fluxes as 0.5 Old + 0.5 New]:::crank

    EXB[Compute TP from old neighbors]:::explicit
    IMB[Form aP TP = aW TW + aE TE + aP0 TP0 + bP]:::implicit
    CNB[Form CN aP TP = 0.5 aW TW + ... + aP0 TP0 + bP]:::crank

    EXC[Stability Check: dt less than dx squared by 2 alpha]:::explicit
    IMC[Unconditional Stability: any dt allowed]:::implicit
    CNC[Oscillation Watch: dt less than dx squared by alpha]:::crank

    ADV[Advance Time: t = t + dt]:::common
    CONV[Check Steady State or End Time]:::common
    OUT[Temperature Field Output]:::common

    INIT --> EX
    INIT --> IM
    INIT --> CN
    EX --> EXA --> EXB --> EXC --> ADV
    IM --> IMA --> IMB --> IMC --> ADV
    CN --> CNA --> CNB --> CNC --> ADV
    ADV --> CONV
    CONV -->|Continue| INIT
    CONV -->|Stop| OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The diagram illustrates the *parallel* structure of the three schemes — they share the initialization and time-advancement logic but differ in *where* the spatial fluxes are evaluated. This is the core conceptual distinction KTU examiners test.
